title: The Beatles

* [Polydor](/wiki/Polydor "Polydor") * [Parlophone](/wiki/Parlophone "Parlophone") * [Tollie](/wiki/Tollie "Tollie") * [Vee-Jay](/wiki/Vee-Jay "Vee-Jay") * [Capitol](/wiki/Capitol_Records "Capitol Records") * [Swan](/wiki/Swan_Records "Swan Records") * [Odeon](/wiki/Odeon_Records "Odeon Records") * [MGM](/wiki/MGM_Records "MGM Records") * [Atco](/wiki/Atco_Records "Atco Records") * [United Artists](/wiki/United_Artists_Records "United Artists Records") * [Apple](/wiki/Apple_Records "Apple Records")
[Full list](/wiki/List_of_awards_and_nominations_received_by_the_Beatles "List of awards and nominations received by the Beatles")

* [John Lennon](/wiki/John_Lennon "John Lennon") * [Paul McCartney](/wiki/Paul_McCartney "Paul McCartney") * [George Harrison](/wiki/George_Harrison "George Harrison") * [Ringo Starr](/wiki/Ringo_Starr "Ringo Starr") * *(see [§ Band members](#Band_members) for others)*

were an English [rock](/wiki/Rock_music "Rock music") band formed in [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool "Liverpool") in 1960. The core lineup of the band comprised [John Lennon](/wiki/John_Lennon "John Lennon"), [Paul McCartney](/wiki/Paul_McCartney "Paul McCartney"), [George Harrison](/wiki/George_Harrison "George Harrison") and [Ringo Starr](/wiki/Ringo_Starr "Ringo Starr"). They are [widely regarded as the most influential band](/wiki/Cultural_impact_of_the_Beatles "Cultural impact of the Beatles") in [popular music](/wiki/Popular_music "Popular music") and were integral to the development of [1960s counterculture](/wiki/1960s_counterculture "1960s counterculture") and the recognition of popular music as an art form.[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHasted2017425-2)[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFrontani2007125-3) Rooted in [skiffle](/wiki/Skiffle "Skiffle"), [beat](/wiki/Beat_music "Beat music") and 1950s [rock 'n' roll](/wiki/Rock_%27n%27_roll "Rock 'n' roll"), their sound incorporated elements of [classical music](/wiki/Classical_music "Classical music") and [traditional pop](/wiki/Traditional_pop "Traditional pop") in innovative ways. The band also explored music styles ranging from [folk](/wiki/Folk_music "Folk music") and [Indian music](/wiki/Indian_music "Indian music") to [psychedelia](/wiki/Psychedelic_music "Psychedelic music") and [hard rock](/wiki/Hard_rock "Hard rock"). [As pioneers in recording](/wiki/Recording_practices_of_the_Beatles "Recording practices of the Beatles"), songwriting and artistic presentation, the Beatles revolutionised many aspects of the music industry and were often publicised as leaders of the [era's youth](/wiki/Baby_boomers "Baby boomers") and sociocultural movements.[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFrontani2007157-4)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

English rock band (1960–1970)

This article is about the band. For their eponymous album, see [*The Beatles* (album)](/wiki/The_Beatles_(album) "The Beatles (album)"). For other uses, see [Beatles (disambiguation)](/wiki/Beatles_(disambiguation) "Beatles (disambiguation)").

"Beatle" and "Fab Four" redirect here. For the insect, see [Beetle](/wiki/Beetle "Beetle"). For other uses, see [Fab Four (disambiguation)](/wiki/Fab_Four_(disambiguation) "Fab Four (disambiguation)").

**The Beatles** were an English [rock](/wiki/Rock_music "Rock music") band formed in [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool "Liverpool") in 1960. The core lineup of the band comprised [John Lennon](/wiki/John_Lennon "John Lennon"), [Paul McCartney](/wiki/Paul_McCartney "Paul McCartney"), [George Harrison](/wiki/George_Harrison "George Harrison") and [Ringo Starr](/wiki/Ringo_Starr "Ringo Starr"). They are [widely regarded as the most influential band](/wiki/Cultural_impact_of_the_Beatles "Cultural impact of the Beatles") in [popular music](/wiki/Popular_music "Popular music") and were integral to the development of [1960s counterculture](/wiki/1960s_counterculture "1960s counterculture") and the recognition of popular music as an art form.[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHasted2017425-2)[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFrontani2007125-3) Rooted in [skiffle](/wiki/Skiffle "Skiffle"), [beat](/wiki/Beat_music "Beat music") and 1950s [rock 'n' roll](/wiki/Rock_%27n%27_roll "Rock 'n' roll"), their sound incorporated elements of [classical music](/wiki/Classical_music "Classical music") and [traditional pop](/wiki/Traditional_pop "Traditional pop") in innovative ways. The band also explored music styles ranging from [folk](/wiki/Folk_music "Folk music") and [Indian music](/wiki/Indian_music "Indian music") to [psychedelia](/wiki/Psychedelic_music "Psychedelic music") and [hard rock](/wiki/Hard_rock "Hard rock"). [As pioneers in recording](/wiki/Recording_practices_of_the_Beatles "Recording practices of the Beatles"), songwriting and artistic presentation, the Beatles revolutionised many aspects of the music industry and were often publicised as leaders of the [era's youth](/wiki/Baby_boomers "Baby boomers") and sociocultural movements.[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFrontani2007157-4)

Led by primary songwriters [Lennon–McCartney](/wiki/Lennon%E2%80%93McCartney "Lennon–McCartney"), the Beatles evolved from Lennon's previous group, [the Quarrymen](/wiki/The_Quarrymen "The Quarrymen"), and built their reputation by playing clubs in Liverpool and [Hamburg](/wiki/Hamburg "Hamburg"), Germany, starting in 1960. The core trio of Lennon, McCartney and Harrison, together since 1958, went through a succession of drummers before inviting Starr to join them in 1962. Manager [Brian Epstein](/wiki/Brian_Epstein "Brian Epstein") moulded them into a professional act, and producer [George Martin](/wiki/George_Martin "George Martin") developed their recordings, greatly expanding their domestic success after they signed with [EMI](/wiki/EMI "EMI") and achieved their first hit, "[Love Me Do](/wiki/Love_Me_Do "Love Me Do")", in late 1962. As their popularity grew into the intense fan frenzy dubbed "[Beatlemania](/wiki/Beatlemania "Beatlemania")", the band acquired the nickname "the Fab Four". By early 1964, the Beatles were international stars and had achieved unprecedented levels of critical and commercial success. They became a leading force in Britain's cultural resurgence, ushering in the [British Invasion](/wiki/British_Invasion "British Invasion") of the United States pop market. They soon made their film debut with *[A Hard Day's Night](/wiki/A_Hard_Day%27s_Night_(film) "A Hard Day's Night (film)")* (1964).

A growing desire to refine their studio efforts, coupled with the challenging nature of their concert tours, led to the band's retirement from live performances in 1966. During this time, they produced albums of greater sophistication, including *[Rubber Soul](/wiki/Rubber_Soul "Rubber Soul")* (1965), *[Revolver](/wiki/Revolver_(Beatles_album) "Revolver (Beatles album)")* (1966) and *[Sgt. Pepper's Lonely Hearts Club Band](/wiki/Sgt._Pepper%27s_Lonely_Hearts_Club_Band "Sgt. Pepper's Lonely Hearts Club Band")* (1967). They enjoyed further commercial success with *[The Beatles](/wiki/The_Beatles_(album) "The Beatles (album)")* (also known as "the White Album", 1968) and *[Abbey Road](/wiki/Abbey_Road "Abbey Road")* (1969). The success of these records heralded the [album era](/wiki/Album_era "Album era"), increased public interest in [psychedelic drugs](/wiki/Psychedelic_drug "Psychedelic drug") and Eastern spirituality, and furthered advancements in [electronic music](/wiki/Electronic_music "Electronic music"), [album art](/wiki/Album_art "Album art") and [music videos](/wiki/Music_video "Music video"). In 1968, they founded [Apple Corps](/wiki/Apple_Corps "Apple Corps"), a multi-armed multimedia corporation that continues to oversee projects related to the band's legacy. After [the group's break-up](/wiki/Break-up_of_the_Beatles "Break-up of the Beatles") in 1970, all principal former members enjoyed success as solo artists. While some [partial reunions](/wiki/Collaborations_between_ex-Beatles "Collaborations between ex-Beatles") occurred over the next decade, the four members never fully reunited. [Lennon was murdered](/wiki/Murder_of_John_Lennon "Murder of John Lennon") in 1980 and Harrison died of lung cancer in 2001; McCartney and Starr remain musically active.

The Beatles are the [best-selling music act of all time](/wiki/List_of_best-selling_music_artists "List of best-selling music artists"), with estimated sales of over 600 million units worldwide.[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) They are the most successful act in the history of the US [*Billboard* charts](/wiki/Billboard_charts "Billboard charts"),[[7]](#cite_note-7) with the [most number-one singles](/wiki/List_of_Billboard_Hot_100_chart_achievements_and_milestones#Most_number-one_songs "List of Billboard Hot 100 chart achievements and milestones") on the US [*Billboard* Hot 100](/wiki/Billboard_Hot_100 "Billboard Hot 100") chart (20). They hold the record for most singles sold in the UK (21.9 million), and held the record for most number-one albums on the [UK Albums Chart](/wiki/UK_Albums_Chart "UK Albums Chart") (15) until [Robbie Williams](/wiki/Robbie_Williams "Robbie Williams") surpassed them in 2026.[[8]](#cite_note-8) The band received [many accolades](/wiki/List_of_awards_and_nominations_received_by_the_Beatles "List of awards and nominations received by the Beatles"), including eight [Grammy Awards](/wiki/Grammy_Awards "Grammy Awards"), four [Brit Awards](/wiki/Brit_Awards "Brit Awards"), an [Academy Award](/wiki/Academy_Award "Academy Award") (for [Best Original Song Score](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Original_Score#1970s "Academy Award for Best Original Score") for the 1970 documentary film *[Let It Be](/wiki/Let_It_Be_(1970_film) "Let It Be (1970 film)")*) and fifteen [Ivor Novello Awards](/wiki/Ivor_Novello_Awards "Ivor Novello Awards"). They were inducted into the [Rock and Roll Hall of Fame](/wiki/Rock_and_Roll_Hall_of_Fame "Rock and Roll Hall of Fame") in their first year of eligibility, 1988, and each principal member was individually inducted between 1994 and 2015. In 2004 and 2011, the group topped *[Rolling Stone](/wiki/Rolling_Stone "Rolling Stone")*'s lists of [the greatest artists in history](/wiki/Rolling_Stone%27s_100_Greatest_Artists_of_All_Time "Rolling Stone's 100 Greatest Artists of All Time"). [*Time* magazine](/wiki/Time_(magazine) "Time (magazine)") named them among the [20th century's 100 most important people](/wiki/Time_100:_The_Most_Important_People_of_the_Century "Time 100: The Most Important People of the Century").

### 1956–1963: Formation

#### The Quarrymen and name changes

Main article: [The Quarrymen](/wiki/The_Quarrymen "The Quarrymen")

In November 1956, sixteen-year-old [John Lennon](/wiki/John_Lennon "John Lennon") formed a [skiffle](/wiki/Skiffle "Skiffle") group with several friends from [Quarry Bank High School](/wiki/Quarry_Bank_High_School "Quarry Bank High School") in [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool "Liverpool"). They were called *[the Quarrymen](/wiki/The_Quarrymen "The Quarrymen")*, a reference to their school song "Quarry men old before our birth".[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn2013104-9) Fifteen-year-old [Paul McCartney](/wiki/Paul_McCartney "Paul McCartney") met Lennon on 6 July 1957 and joined as a [rhythm guitarist](/wiki/Rhythm_guitar "Rhythm guitar") shortly after.[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpitz200593–99-10) In February 1958, McCartney invited his friend [George Harrison](/wiki/George_Harrison "George Harrison"), then aged fifteen, to watch the band. Harrison auditioned for Lennon, impressing him with his playing, but Lennon initially thought Harrison was too young. After a month's persistence, during a second meeting (arranged by McCartney), Harrison performed the lead guitar part of the instrumental song "[Raunchy](/wiki/Raunchy_(instrumental) "Raunchy (instrumental)")" on the upper deck of a Liverpool bus,[[11]](#cite_note-11) and they enlisted him as [lead guitarist](/wiki/Lead_guitar "Lead guitar").[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiles199747-12)[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn199213-13)

By January 1959, Lennon's Quarry Bank friends had left the group and he began his studies at the [Liverpool College of Art](/wiki/Liverpool_College_of_Art "Liverpool College of Art").[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarry2000a103-14) The three guitarists, billing themselves as Johnny and the Moondogs,[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn199217-15) were playing [rock and roll](/wiki/Rock_and_roll "Rock and roll") whenever they could find a drummer.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarry2000b742–743-16) They also performed as *the Rainbows*. Paul McCartney later told *New Musical Express* that they called themselves that "because we all had different coloured shirts and we couldn't afford any others!"[[17]](#cite_note-17)

Lennon's art school friend [Stuart Sutcliffe](/wiki/Stuart_Sutcliffe "Stuart Sutcliffe"), who had just sold one of his paintings and was persuaded to purchase a bass guitar with the proceeds, joined in January 1960. He suggested changing the band's name to *Beatals*, as a tribute to [Buddy Holly](/wiki/Buddy_Holly "Buddy Holly") and [the Crickets](/wiki/The_Crickets "The Crickets").[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn199218-18)[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGilliland1969show_27,_track_4-19) They used this name until May, when they became *the Silver Beetles*, before undertaking a brief [tour of Scotland](/wiki/The_Beat_Ballad_Show_Tour "The Beat Ballad Show Tour") as the backing group for pop singer and fellow [Liverpudlian](/wiki/Liverpudlian "Liverpudlian") [Johnny Gentle](/wiki/Johnny_Gentle "Johnny Gentle"). By early July, they had refashioned themselves as *the Silver Beatles* and by the middle of August simply *the Beatles*.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn199218–22-20)

#### Early residencies and UK popularity

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/7/74/The_Beatles_in_Hamburg.webp)](/wiki/File:The_Beatles_in_Hamburg.webp)

Left to right: [Pete Best](/wiki/Pete_Best "Pete Best"), [George Harrison](/wiki/George_Harrison "George Harrison"), [John Lennon](/wiki/John_Lennon "John Lennon"), [Paul McCartney](/wiki/Paul_McCartney "Paul McCartney"), and [Stuart Sutcliffe](/wiki/Stuart_Sutcliffe "Stuart Sutcliffe") at Hamburg Funfair in 1960, photographed by [Astrid Kirchherr](/wiki/Astrid_Kirchherr "Astrid Kirchherr"). Sutcliffe left the Beatles in 1961 and [Ringo Starr](/wiki/Ringo_Starr "Ringo Starr") replaced Best in 1962.

[Allan Williams](/wiki/Allan_Williams "Allan Williams"), the Beatles' unofficial manager, arranged a [residency for them in Hamburg](/wiki/The_Beatles_in_Hamburg "The Beatles in Hamburg"). They auditioned and hired drummer [Pete Best](/wiki/Pete_Best "Pete Best") in mid-August 1960. The band, now a five-piece, departed Liverpool for Hamburg four days later, contracted to club owner [Bruno Koschmider](/wiki/Bruno_Koschmider "Bruno Koschmider") for what would be a 3+1⁄2-month residency.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn199221–25-21) Beatles historian [Mark Lewisohn](/wiki/Mark_Lewisohn "Mark Lewisohn") writes: "They pulled into Hamburg at dusk on 17 August, the time when the [red-light area](/wiki/Red-light_district "Red-light district") comes to life ... flashing neon lights screamed out the various entertainment on offer, while scantily clad women sat unabashed in shop windows waiting for business opportunities."[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn199222-22)

Koschmider had converted a couple of strip clubs in the red light [Reeperbahn](/wiki/Reeperbahn "Reeperbahn") district of [St. Pauli](/wiki/St._Pauli "St. Pauli") into music venues and initially placed the Beatles at the [Indra Club](/wiki/Indra_Club "Indra Club"). After closing Indra due to noise complaints, he moved them to the [Kaiserkeller](/wiki/Kaiserkeller "Kaiserkeller") in October.[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn199223-23) When he learned they had been performing at the rival [Top Ten Club](/wiki/Top_Ten_Club "Top Ten Club") in breach of their contract, he gave them one month's termination notice,[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn199224,_33-24) and reported the underage Harrison, who had obtained permission to stay in Hamburg by lying to the German authorities about his age.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould200788-25) The authorities arranged for Harrison's deportation in late November.[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn199224-26) One week later, Koschmider had McCartney and Best arrested for arson after they set fire to a condom in a concrete corridor; the authorities deported them.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn199224–25-27) Lennon returned to Liverpool in early December, while Sutcliffe remained in Hamburg until late February with his German fiancée [Astrid Kirchherr](/wiki/Astrid_Kirchherr "Astrid Kirchherr"),[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn199225-28) who took the first semi-professional photos of the Beatles.[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpitz2005222–224-29)

During the next two years, the Beatles were [resident for periods in Hamburg](/wiki/The_Beatles_in_Hamburg "The Beatles in Hamburg"), where they used [Preludin](/wiki/Preludin "Preludin") both recreationally and to maintain their energy through all-night performances.[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiles199766–67-30) In 1961, during their second Hamburg engagement, Kirchherr cut Sutcliffe's hair in the "[exi](/wiki/Exi_(subculture) "Exi (subculture)")" (existentialist) style, later adopted by the other Beatles.[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn199232-31)[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiles199776-32) Sutcliffe decided to leave the band early that year and resume his art studies in Germany. McCartney took over bass.[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould200789,_94-33) Producer [Bert Kaempfert](/wiki/Bert_Kaempfert "Bert Kaempfert") contracted what was now a four-piece group until June 1962, and he used them as [Tony Sheridan](/wiki/Tony_Sheridan "Tony Sheridan")'s [backing band](/wiki/Backing_band "Backing band") on a series of recordings for [Polydor Records](/wiki/Polydor_Records "Polydor Records").[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGilliland1969show_27,_track_4-19)[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpitz2005249–251-34) As part of the sessions, the Beatles were signed to Polydor for one year.[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn2013450-35) Credited to "Tony Sheridan & the Beat Brothers", the single "[My Bonnie](/wiki/My_Bonnie_(song) "My Bonnie (song)")", recorded in June 1961 and released four months later, reached number 32 on the *[Musikmarkt](/wiki/Musikmarkt "Musikmarkt")* chart.[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEverett2001100-36)

After the Beatles completed their second [Hamburg residency](/wiki/The_Beatles_in_Hamburg "The Beatles in Hamburg"), they enjoyed increasing popularity in Liverpool with the growing [Merseybeat](/wiki/Merseybeat "Merseybeat") movement. However, they were growing tired of the monotony of numerous appearances at the same clubs night after night.[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn199233-37) In November 1961, during one of the group's frequent performances at [the Cavern Club](/wiki/The_Cavern_Club "The Cavern Club"), they encountered [Brian Epstein](/wiki/Brian_Epstein "Brian Epstein"), a local record-store owner and music columnist.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiles199784–87-38) He later recalled: "I immediately liked what I heard. They were fresh, and they were honest, and they had what I thought was a sort of presence ... [a] star quality."[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn199234–35-39)

#### First EMI recordings

Epstein courted the band over the next couple of months, and they appointed him as their manager in January 1962.[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiles199784–88-40) Throughout early and mid-1962, Epstein sought to free the Beatles from their contractual obligations to Bert Kaempfert Productions. He eventually negotiated a one-month early release in exchange for one last recording session in Hamburg.[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWinn200810-41) On their return to Germany in April, a distraught Kirchherr met them at the airport with news of Sutcliffe's death the previous day from a [brain haemorrhage](/wiki/Brain_haemorrhage "Brain haemorrhage").[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn199256-42) Epstein began negotiations with record labels for a recording contract. To secure a UK record contract, Epstein negotiated an early end to the band's contract with Polydor, in exchange for more recordings backing Tony Sheridan.[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn2013612,_629-43) After a New Year's Day audition, [Decca Records rejected the band](/wiki/The_Beatles%27_Decca_audition "The Beatles' Decca audition"), saying, "Guitar groups are on the way out, Mr. Epstein".[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThe_Beatles200067-44) However, three months later, producer [George Martin](/wiki/George_Martin "George Martin") signed the Beatles to [EMI](/wiki/EMI "EMI")'s [Parlophone](/wiki/Parlophone "Parlophone") label.[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn199256-42)

[![A flight of stone steps leads from an asphalt car park up to the main entrance of a white two-story building. The ground floor has two sash windows, the first floor has three shorter sash windows. Two more windows are visible at basement level. The decorative stonework around the doors and windows is painted grey.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/Abbey_Rd_Studios.jpg/250px-Abbey_Rd_Studios.jpg)](/wiki/File:Abbey_Rd_Studios.jpg)

Main entrance at [EMI Studios](/wiki/EMI_Studios "EMI Studios") (now Abbey Road Studios, pictured 2007)

Martin's first recording session with the Beatles took place at [EMI Recording Studios](/wiki/EMI_Recording_Studios "EMI Recording Studios") (later Abbey Road Studios) in London on 6 June 1962.[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn199259-45) He immediately complained to Epstein about Best's drumming and suggested they use a [session drummer](/wiki/Session_drummer "Session drummer") in his place.[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpitz2005318,_322-46) Already contemplating Best's dismissal,[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiles199849–50-47) the Beatles replaced him in mid-August with [Ringo Starr](/wiki/Ringo_Starr "Ringo Starr"), who left [Rory Storm and the Hurricanes](/wiki/Rory_Storm_and_the_Hurricanes "Rory Storm and the Hurricanes") to join them.[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn199259-45) A 4 September session at EMI yielded a recording of "[Love Me Do](/wiki/Love_Me_Do "Love Me Do")" featuring Starr on drums, but a dissatisfied Martin hired drummer [Andy White](/wiki/Andy_White_(drummer) "Andy White (drummer)") for the band's third session a week later, which produced recordings of "Love Me Do", "[Please Please Me](/wiki/Please_Please_Me_(song) "Please Please Me (song)")" and "[P.S. I Love You](/wiki/P.S._I_Love_You_(Beatles_song) "P.S. I Love You (Beatles song)")".[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn199259-45)

Martin initially selected the Starr version of "Love Me Do" for the band's first single, though subsequent re-pressings featured the White version, with Starr on tambourine.[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn199259-45) Released in early October, "Love Me Do" peaked at number 17 on the *[Record Retailer](/wiki/Record_Retailer "Record Retailer")* chart.[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn199259–60-48) Their television debut came later that month with a live performance on the regional news programme *[People and Places](/wiki/Granada_Reports "Granada Reports")*.[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn199281,_355-49) After Martin suggested rerecording "Please Please Me" at a faster tempo,[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThe_Beatles200090-50) a studio session in late November yielded that recording,[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn199262,_84-51) of which Martin accurately predicted, "You've just made your first No. 1".[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarry2000a875-52)

In December 1962, the Beatles concluded their fifth and final Hamburg residency at the [Star-Club](/wiki/Star-Club "Star-Club").[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn199262,_86-53) By 1963, they had agreed that all four band members would contribute vocals to their albums – including Starr, despite his restricted vocal range, to validate his standing in the group.[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007191-54) Lennon and McCartney had established [a songwriting partnership](/wiki/Lennon%E2%80%93McCartney "Lennon–McCartney"), and as the band's success grew, their dominant collaboration limited Harrison's opportunities as a [lead vocalist](/wiki/Lead_vocalist "Lead vocalist").[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarry2000a494-55) Epstein, to maximise the Beatles' commercial potential, encouraged them to adopt a professional approach to performing.[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007128,_133–134-56) Lennon recalled him saying, "Look, if you really want to get in these bigger places, you're going to have to change – stop eating on stage, stop swearing, stop smoking ...".[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThe_Beatles200067-44)[[nb 1]](#cite_note-57)

In May 2024, Starr downplayed the significance of his role with the Beatles and said that he never regularly practiced with them, telling [Dan Rather](/wiki/Dan_Rather "Dan Rather") "I hate practicing. I hate sitting there" and in fact often looked for inspiration from other musicians.[[57]](#cite_note-starrrathermay2024-58)

### 1963–1966: Beatlemania and touring years

Main article: [Beatlemania](/wiki/Beatlemania "Beatlemania")

#### *Please Please Me* and *With the Beatles*

[![The logo of the English rock band the Beatles](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/33/The_Beatles_logo.svg/250px-The_Beatles_logo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:The_Beatles_logo.svg)

The band's logo was designed by [Ivor Arbiter](/wiki/Ivor_Arbiter "Ivor Arbiter").[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWomack200776-59)

On 11 February 1963, the Beatles recorded ten songs during a single studio session for their debut LP, *[Please Please Me](/wiki/Please_Please_Me "Please Please Me")*. It was supplemented by the four tracks already released on their first two singles. Martin considered recording the LP live at [The Cavern Club](/wiki/The_Cavern_Club "The Cavern Club"), but after deciding that the building's acoustics were inadequate, he elected to simulate a "live" album with minimal production in "a single marathon session at [Abbey Road](/wiki/Abbey_Road_Studios "Abbey Road Studios")".[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007147-60) After the moderate success of "Love Me Do", the single "Please Please Me" was released in January 1963, two months ahead of the album. It reached number one on every UK chart except *Record Retailer*, where it peaked at number two.[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn199288,_351-61)

Recalling how the Beatles "rushed to deliver a debut album, bashing out *Please Please Me* in a day", [AllMusic](/wiki/AllMusic "AllMusic") critic [Stephen Thomas Erlewine](/wiki/Stephen_Thomas_Erlewine "Stephen Thomas Erlewine") wrote: "Decades after its release, the album still sounds fresh, precisely because of its intense origins."[[61]](#cite_note-Please_Please_Me_AllMusic-62) Lennon said little thought went into composition at the time; he and McCartney were "just writing songs *à la* [Everly Brothers](/wiki/Everly_Brothers "Everly Brothers"), *à la* Buddy Holly, pop songs with no more thought of them than that – to create a sound. And the words were almost irrelevant."[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESheff1981129-63)

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Gnome-mime-audio-openclipart.svg/60px-Gnome-mime-audio-openclipart.svg.png)

["She Loves You"](/wiki/File:She_Loves_You_(Beatles_song_-_sample).ogg "File:She Loves You (Beatles song - sample).ogg")

Sample of "[She Loves You](/wiki/She_Loves_You "She Loves You")". The song's repeated use of "yeah" exclamations became a signature phrase for the group at the time.[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDavies1968200-64)[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn198835-65)

---

*Problems playing this file? See [media help](/wiki/Help:Media "Help:Media").*

Released in March 1963, *Please Please Me* was the first of eleven consecutive Beatles albums released in the United Kingdom to reach number one.[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn199290,_351-66) The band's third single, "[From Me to You](/wiki/From_Me_to_You "From Me to You")", came out in April and began an almost unbroken string of seventeen British number-one singles, including all but one of the eighteen they released over the next six years.[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn199289,_350–351-67) Issued in August, their fourth single, "[She Loves You](/wiki/She_Loves_You "She Loves You")", achieved the fastest sales of any record in the UK up to that time, selling three-quarters of a million copies in under four weeks.[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007159-68) It became their first single to sell a million copies and remained the biggest-selling record in the UK until 1978.[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarry2000a990-69)[[nb 2]](#cite_note-70)

The success brought increased media exposure, to which the Beatles responded with an irreverent and comical attitude that defied the expectations of pop musicians at the time, inspiring even more interest.[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007166–169-71) The band toured the UK three times in the first half of the year: a four-week tour that began in February, the Beatles' first nationwide, preceded three-week tours in March and May–June.[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn199290,_98–105,_109–112-72) As their popularity spread, a frenzied adulation of the group took hold. On 13 October, the Beatles starred on *[Sunday Night at the London Palladium](/wiki/Sunday_Night_at_the_London_Palladium "Sunday Night at the London Palladium")*, the UK's top variety show.[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPawlowski1990146-73) Their performance was televised live and watched by 15 million viewers. One national paper's headlines in the following days coined the term "[Beatlemania](/wiki/Beatlemania "Beatlemania")" to describe the riotous enthusiasm by screaming fans who greeted the band – and it stuck.[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPawlowski1990146-73)[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpitz2005444–445-74) Although not billed as tour leaders, the Beatles overshadowed American acts [Tommy Roe](/wiki/Tommy_Roe "Tommy Roe") and [Chris Montez](/wiki/Chris_Montez "Chris Montez") during the February engagements and assumed top billing "by audience demand", something no British act had previously accomplished while touring with artists from the US.[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn199288-75) A similar situation arose during their May–June [tour with Roy Orbison](/wiki/Roy_Orbison/The_Beatles_Tour "Roy Orbison/The Beatles Tour").[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn199290-76)

[![Paul McCartney, George Harrison, Swedish pop singer Lill-Babs and John Lennon on the set of the Swedish television show Drop-In in 1963](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3a/The_Beatles_and_Lill-Babs_1963.jpg/250px-The_Beatles_and_Lill-Babs_1963.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Beatles_and_Lill-Babs_1963.jpg)

McCartney, Harrison, Swedish pop singer [Lill-Babs](/wiki/Lill-Babs "Lill-Babs") and Lennon on the set of the [Swedish television](/wiki/Sveriges_Television "Sveriges Television") show *Drop-In*, 30 October 1963[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiles199886-77)

In late October, the Beatles began a five-day tour of Sweden, their first time abroad since the final Hamburg engagement of December 1962.[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarry2000a1088-78) On their return to the UK on 31 October, several hundred screaming fans greeted them in heavy rain at [Heathrow Airport](/wiki/Heathrow_Airport "Heathrow Airport"). Around 50 to 100 journalists and photographers, as well as representatives from the [BBC](/wiki/BBC_Television "BBC Television"), also joined the airport reception, the first of more than 100 such events.[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn199292–93-79) The next day, the band began its fourth tour of Britain within nine months, this one scheduled for six weeks.[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn1992127–133-80) In mid-November, as Beatlemania intensified, police resorted to using high-pressure water hoses to control the crowd before a concert in Plymouth.[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDavies1968184–185-81) On 4 November, they played in front of [The Queen Mother](/wiki/The_Queen_Mother "The Queen Mother") and [Princess Margaret](/wiki/Princess_Margaret "Princess Margaret") during the [Royal Variety Performance](/wiki/List_of_Royal_Variety_Performances#1960s "List of Royal Variety Performances") at the [Prince of Wales Theatre](/wiki/Prince_of_Wales_Theatre "Prince of Wales Theatre").[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiles2001111-82)

*Please Please Me* maintained the top position on the *Record Retailer* chart for 30 weeks, only to be displaced by its follow-up, *[With the Beatles](/wiki/With_the_Beatles "With the Beatles")*,[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn199290,_92,_100-83) which EMI released on 22 November to record advance orders of 270,000 copies. The LP topped a half-million albums sold in one week.[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn199293-84) Recorded between July and October, *With the Beatles* made better use of studio production techniques than its predecessor.[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007187-85) It held the top spot for 21 weeks with a chart life of 40 weeks.[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarry2000a1161-86) Erlewine described the LP as "a sequel of the highest order – one that betters the original".[[85]](#cite_note-87)

In a reversal of then standard practice, EMI released the album ahead of the impending single "[I Want to Hold Your Hand](/wiki/I_Want_to_Hold_Your_Hand "I Want to Hold Your Hand")", with the song excluded to maximise the single's sales.[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007187–188-88) The album caught the attention of music critic [William Mann](/wiki/William_Mann_(critic) "William Mann (critic)") of *[The Times](/wiki/The_Times "The Times")*, who suggested that Lennon and McCartney were "the outstanding English composers of 1963".[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007187-85) The newspaper published a series of articles in which Mann offered detailed analyses of the music, lending it respectability.[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarry2000a1162-89) *With the Beatles* became the second album in UK chart history to sell a million copies, a figure previously reached only by the 1958 [*South Pacific* soundtrack](/wiki/South_Pacific_(soundtrack) "South Pacific (soundtrack)").[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarry2000b978-90) When writing the [sleeve notes](/wiki/Sleeve_notes "Sleeve notes") for the album, the band's press officer, [Tony Barrow](/wiki/Tony_Barrow "Tony Barrow"), used the superlative the "fabulous foursome", which the media widely adopted as "the Fab Four".[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarry2000a402-91)

#### First visit to the United States and the British Invasion

For broader coverage of this topic, see [British Invasion](/wiki/British_Invasion "British Invasion").

[![A black-and-white image of four men standing in front of a crowd of people at the bottom of an aeroplane staircase](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/61/The_Beatles_arrive_at_JFK_Airport.jpg/250px-The_Beatles_arrive_at_JFK_Airport.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Beatles_arrive_at_JFK_Airport.jpg)

The Beatles arriving at [John F. Kennedy International Airport](/wiki/John_F._Kennedy_International_Airport "John F. Kennedy International Airport"), 7 February 1964

EMI's American subsidiary, [Capitol Records](/wiki/Capitol_Records "Capitol Records"), hindered the Beatles' releases in the United States for more than a year by initially declining to issue their music, including their first three singles. Concurrent negotiations with the independent US label [Vee-Jay](/wiki/Vee-Jay "Vee-Jay") led to the release of some, but not all, of the songs in 1963.[[90]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn1992350-92) Vee-Jay finished preparation for the album *[Introducing... The Beatles](/wiki/Introducing..._The_Beatles "Introducing... The Beatles")*, comprising most of the songs of Parlophone's *Please Please Me*, but a management shake-up led to the album not being released.[[nb 3]](#cite_note-94) After it emerged that the label did not report royalties on their sales, the licence that Vee-Jay had signed with EMI was voided.[[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpizer200440-95) A new licence was granted to the [Swan](/wiki/Swan_Records "Swan Records") label for the single "She Loves You". The record received some airplay in the [Tidewater area](/wiki/Tidewater_region "Tidewater region") of Virginia from Gene Loving of radio station [WGH](/wiki/WGH_(AM) "WGH (AM)") and was featured on the "Rate-a-Record" segment of *[American Bandstand](/wiki/American_Bandstand "American Bandstand")*, but it failed to catch on nationally.[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarry2000a225–226,_228,_1118–1122-96)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/Beatlemania_fan_-_Press_and_Sun-Bulletin_%281964%29.jpg/250px-Beatlemania_fan_-_Press_and_Sun-Bulletin_%281964%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Beatlemania_fan_-_Press_and_Sun-Bulletin_(1964).jpg)

A newspaper clipping from 8 February 1964 covering "Beatlemania"

Epstein brought a demo copy of "[I Want to Hold Your Hand](/wiki/I_Want_to_Hold_Your_Hand "I Want to Hold Your Hand")" to Capitol's [Brown Meggs](/wiki/Brown_Meggs "Brown Meggs"), who signed the band and arranged for a $40,000 US marketing campaign. American chart success began after disc jockey Carroll James of AM radio station [WWDC](/wiki/WQOF "WQOF"), in Washington, DC, obtained a copy of the British single "I Want to Hold Your Hand" in mid-December 1963 and began playing it on-air.[[94]](#cite_note-97) Taped copies of the song soon circulated among other radio stations throughout the US. This caused an increase in demand, leading Capitol to bring forward the release of "I Want to Hold Your Hand" by three weeks.[[95]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEverett2001206-98) Issued on 26 December, with the band's previously scheduled debut there just weeks away, "I Want to Hold Your Hand" sold a million copies, becoming a number-one hit in the US by mid-January.[[96]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn1992136,_350-99) In its wake Vee-Jay released *Introducing... The Beatles*[[97]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpizer200496-100) along with Capitol's debut album, *[Meet the Beatles!](/wiki/Meet_the_Beatles! "Meet the Beatles!")*, while Swan reactivated production of "She Loves You".[[98]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDavies1968218-101) The [Odeon](/wiki/Odeon_Records "Odeon Records") label, an EMI subsidiary, pressed Beatles LPs to be shipped out and distributed abroad.[[99]](#cite_note-102) After the Beatles' US trip, [MGM Records](/wiki/MGM_Records "MGM Records") released the single "My Bonnie" backed with "The Saints" in the US on 27 January 1964 as a Beatles single,[[100]](#cite_note-103) and [Atco](/wiki/Atco_Records "Atco Records") followed in issuing the recordings the Beatles had made in Germany with Tony Sheridan in 1961 and 1962.[[101]](#cite_note-104)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/5/56/Beatles_with_Ed_Sullivan.jpg/250px-Beatles_with_Ed_Sullivan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Beatles_with_Ed_Sullivan.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/51/The_Beatles_performing_at_The_Ed_Sullivan_Show_%28cropped_2%29.jpg/250px-The_Beatles_performing_at_The_Ed_Sullivan_Show_%28cropped_2%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Beatles_performing_at_The_Ed_Sullivan_Show_(cropped_2).jpg)

The Beatles performing on *[The Ed Sullivan Show](/wiki/The_Ed_Sullivan_Show "The Ed Sullivan Show")*, February 1964

On 7 February 1964, the Beatles departed from Heathrow with an estimated 4,000 fans waving and screaming as the aircraft took off.[[102]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpitz2005457-105) Upon landing at New York's [John F. Kennedy Airport](/wiki/John_F._Kennedy_Airport "John F. Kennedy Airport"), an uproarious crowd estimated at 3,000 greeted them.[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpitz2005459-106) They gave their first live US television performance two days later on *[The Ed Sullivan Show](/wiki/The_Beatles_on_The_Ed_Sullivan_Show "The Beatles on The Ed Sullivan Show")*, watched by approximately 73 million viewers in over 23 million households,[[104]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn1992137-107) or 34 per cent of the American population. Biographer Jonathan Gould writes that, according to the [Nielsen](/wiki/Nielsen_ratings "Nielsen ratings") rating service, it was "the largest audience that had ever been recorded for an American television program".[[105]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould20073-108) The next morning, the Beatles awoke to a largely negative critical consensus in the US,[[106]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpitz2005473–474-109) but a day later at their first US concert, Beatlemania erupted at the [Washington Coliseum](/wiki/Washington_Coliseum "Washington Coliseum").[[107]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarry2000a1134–1135-110) Back in New York the following day, the Beatles met with another strong reception during two shows at [Carnegie Hall](/wiki/Carnegie_Hall "Carnegie Hall").[[104]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn1992137-107) The band flew to Florida, where they appeared on *The Ed Sullivan Show* a second time, again before 70 million viewers, before returning to the UK on 22 February.[[108]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn1992137,_146–147-111)

The Beatles' first visit to the US took place when the nation was still mourning the [assassination of President John F. Kennedy](/wiki/Assassination_of_President_John_F._Kennedy "Assassination of President John F. Kennedy") the previous November.[[109]](#cite_note-Gimore_(RS_Beatles/Dylan/60s)-112) Commentators often suggest that for many, particularly the young, the Beatles' performances reignited the sense of excitement and possibility that had momentarily faded in the wake of the assassination and helped pave the way for the revolutionary social changes to come later in the decade.[[110]](#cite_note-113) Their hairstyle, unusually long for the era and mocked by many adults,[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGilliland1969show_27,_track_4-19) became an emblem of rebellion to the burgeoning youth culture.[[111]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007345-114)

The group's popularity generated unprecedented interest in British music, and many other UK acts subsequently made their American debuts, successfully touring over the next three years in what was termed the [British Invasion](/wiki/British_Invasion "British Invasion").[[112]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould20079,_250,_285-115) The Beatles' success in the US opened the door for a successive string of British [beat groups](/wiki/Beat_music "Beat music") and [pop](/wiki/Pop_music "Pop music") acts such as [the Dave Clark Five](/wiki/The_Dave_Clark_Five "The Dave Clark Five"), [the Animals](/wiki/The_Animals "The Animals"), [Herman's Hermits](/wiki/Herman%27s_Hermits "Herman's Hermits"), [Petula Clark](/wiki/Petula_Clark "Petula Clark"), [the Kinks](/wiki/The_Kinks "The Kinks") and [the Rolling Stones](/wiki/The_Rolling_Stones "The Rolling Stones") to achieve success in America.[[113]](#cite_note-Puterbaugh_(British_Invasion)-116) During the week of 4 April 1964, the Beatles held twelve positions on the [*Billboard* Hot 100](/wiki/Billboard_Hot_100 "Billboard Hot 100") singles chart, including the top five.[[114]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn1992138-117)[[nb 4]](#cite_note-119)

#### *A Hard Day's Night*

Main article: [*A Hard Day's Night* (film)](/wiki/A_Hard_Day%27s_Night_(film) "A Hard Day's Night (film)")

Capitol Records' lack of interest throughout 1963 did not go unnoticed, and a competitor, [United Artists Records](/wiki/United_Artists_Records "United Artists Records"), encouraged [its film division](/wiki/United_Artists "United Artists") to offer the Beatles a three-motion-picture deal, primarily for the commercial potential of the soundtracks in the US.[[116]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarry2000a483–484-120) Directed by [Richard Lester](/wiki/Richard_Lester "Richard Lester"), *[A Hard Day's Night](/wiki/A_Hard_Day%27s_Night_(film) "A Hard Day's Night (film)")* involved the band for six weeks in March–April 1964 as they played themselves in a musical comedy.[[117]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007230–232-121) The film premiered in London and New York in July and August, respectively, and was an international success, with some critics drawing a comparison with the [Marx Brothers](/wiki/Marx_Brothers "Marx Brothers").[[118]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarry2000a489–490-122)

United Artists released a [full soundtrack album](/wiki/A_Hard_Day%27s_Night_(album)#North_American_release "A Hard Day's Night (album)") for the North American market, combining Beatles songs and Martin's orchestral score; elsewhere, the group's third studio LP, *[A Hard Day's Night](/wiki/A_Hard_Day%27s_Night_(album) "A Hard Day's Night (album)")*, contained songs from the film on side one and other new recordings on side two.[[119]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn198847-123) According to Erlewine, the album saw them "truly coming into their own as a band. All of the disparate influences on their first two albums coalesced into a bright, joyous, original sound, filled with ringing guitars and irresistible melodies."[[120]](#cite_note-124) That "ringing guitar" sound was primarily the product of Harrison's [12-string electric Rickenbacker](/wiki/Rickenbacker_360/12 "Rickenbacker 360/12"), a prototype given to him by the manufacturer, which made its debut on the record.[[121]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007286–287-125)[[nb 5]](#cite_note-126)

#### 1964 world tour, meeting Bob Dylan and stand on civil rights

[![Paul McCartney, George Harrison and John Lennon of the Beatles performing on Dutch TV in 1964](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/04/Paul%2C_George_%26_John.png/330px-Paul%2C_George_%26_John.png)](/wiki/File:Paul,_George_%26_John.png)

McCartney, Harrison and Lennon performing on Dutch TV in 1964

Touring internationally in June and July, the Beatles staged 37 shows over 27 days in Denmark, the Netherlands, Hong Kong, Australia and New Zealand.[[122]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn1992161–165-127)[[nb 6]](#cite_note-129) In August and September, they returned to the US, with a 30-concert tour of 23 cities.[[124]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007249-130) Generating intense interest once again, the month-long tour attracted between 10,000 and 20,000 people to each 30-minute performance in cities from New York to San Francisco.[[124]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007249-130)

In August, journalist [Al Aronowitz](/wiki/Al_Aronowitz "Al Aronowitz") arranged for the Beatles to meet [Bob Dylan](/wiki/Bob_Dylan "Bob Dylan").[[125]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007252-131) Visiting the band in their New York hotel suite, Dylan introduced them to [cannabis](/wiki/Cannabis_(drug) "Cannabis (drug)").[[126]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiles1997185-132) Gould points out the musical and cultural significance of this meeting, before which the musicians' respective fan bases were "perceived as inhabiting two separate subcultural worlds": Dylan's audience of "college kids with artistic or intellectual leanings, a dawning political and social idealism, and a mildly [bohemian style](/wiki/Bohemian_style "Bohemian style")" contrasted with their fans, "veritable '[teenyboppers](/wiki/Teenybopper "Teenybopper")' – kids in high school or grade school whose lives were totally wrapped up in the commercialised popular culture of television, radio, pop records, fan magazines and teen fashion. To many of Dylan's followers in the [folk music](/wiki/Folk_music "Folk music") scene, the Beatles were seen as idolaters, not idealists."[[127]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007252–253-133)

Within six months of the meeting, according to Gould, "Lennon would be making records on which he openly imitated Dylan's nasal drone, brittle strum, and introspective vocal persona"; and six months after that, Dylan began performing with a backing band and [electric instrumentation](/wiki/Electric_Dylan_controversy "Electric Dylan controversy"), and "dressed in the height of Mod fashion".[[128]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007253-134) As a result, Gould continues, the traditional division between folk and rock enthusiasts "nearly evaporated", as the Beatles' fans began to mature in their outlook and Dylan's audience embraced the new, youth-driven pop culture.[[128]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007253-134)

During [the 1964 US tour](/wiki/The_Beatles%27_1964_North_American_tour "The Beatles' 1964 North American tour"), the group were confronted with [racial segregation](/wiki/Racial_segregation_in_the_United_States "Racial segregation in the United States") in the country at the time.[[129]](#cite_note-Integration_(BBC)-135)[[130]](#cite_note-Integration_(Mirken)-136) When informed that the venue for their 11 September concert, the [Gator Bowl](/wiki/Gator_Bowl_Stadium "Gator Bowl Stadium") in [Jacksonville, Florida](/wiki/Jacksonville,_Florida "Jacksonville, Florida"), was segregated, the Beatles said they would refuse to perform unless the audience was integrated.[[131]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn1992171-137)[[129]](#cite_note-Integration_(BBC)-135)[[130]](#cite_note-Integration_(Mirken)-136) Lennon stated: "We never play to segregated audiences and we aren't going to start now ... I'd sooner lose our appearance money."[[129]](#cite_note-Integration_(BBC)-135) City officials relented and agreed to allow an integrated show.[[129]](#cite_note-Integration_(BBC)-135) The group also cancelled their reservations at the whites-only [Hotel George Washington](/wiki/Hotel_George_Washington "Hotel George Washington") in Jacksonville.[[130]](#cite_note-Integration_(Mirken)-136) For their subsequent US tours in 1965 and 1966, the Beatles included clauses in contracts stipulating that shows be integrated.[[130]](#cite_note-Integration_(Mirken)-136)[[132]](#cite_note-Integration_(Huffpost)-138)

#### *Beatles for Sale*, *Help!* and *Rubber Soul*

According to Gould, the Beatles' fourth studio LP, *[Beatles for Sale](/wiki/Beatles_for_Sale "Beatles for Sale")*, evidenced a growing conflict between the commercial pressures of their global success and their creative ambitions.[[133]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007255–256-139) They had intended the album, recorded between August and October 1964,[[134]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn1992167–176-140) to continue the format established by *A Hard Day's Night* which, unlike their first two LPs, contained only original songs.[[133]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007255–256-139) They had nearly exhausted their backlog of songs on the previous album, however, and given the challenges constant international touring posed to their songwriting efforts, Lennon admitted, "Material's becoming a hell of a problem".[[135]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007256-141) As a result, six covers from their extensive repertoire were chosen to complete the album. Released in early December, its eight original compositions stood out, demonstrating the growing maturity of the [Lennon–McCartney](/wiki/Lennon%E2%80%93McCartney "Lennon–McCartney") songwriting partnership.[[133]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007255–256-139)

In early 1965, following a dinner with Lennon, Harrison and their wives, Harrison's dentist, John Riley, secretly added [LSD](/wiki/LSD "LSD") to their coffee.[[136]](#cite_note-142) Lennon described the experience: "It was just terrifying, but it was fantastic. I was pretty stunned for a month or two."[[137]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007316-143) He and Harrison subsequently became regular users of the drug, joined by Starr on at least one occasion. Harrison's use of [psychedelic drugs](/wiki/Psychedelic_drug "Psychedelic drug") encouraged his path to meditation and Hinduism. He commented: "For me, it was like a flash. The first time I had [acid](/wiki/Lysergic_acid_diethylamide "Lysergic acid diethylamide"), it just opened up something in my head that was inside of me, and I realised a lot of things. I didn't learn them because I already knew them, but that happened to be the key that opened the door to reveal them. From the moment I had that, I wanted to have it all the time – these thoughts about the yogis and the Himalayas, and [Ravi](/wiki/Ravi_Shankar "Ravi Shankar")'s music."[[138]](#cite_note-144)[[139]](#cite_note-145) McCartney was initially reluctant to try it, but eventually did so in late 1966.[[140]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007317-146) He became the first Beatle to discuss LSD publicly, declaring in a magazine interview that "it opened my eyes" and "made me a better, more honest, more tolerant member of society".[[141]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrownGaines2002228-147)

[![The Beatles performing music in a field. In the foreground, the drums are played by Starr (only the top of his head is visible). Beyond him, the other three stand in a column with their guitars. In the rear, Harrison, head down, strikes a chord. In the front, Lennon smiles and gives a little wave toward camera, holding his pick. Between them, McCartney is jocularly about to choke Lennon.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/TrailerUSHelp.jpg/250px-TrailerUSHelp.jpg)](/wiki/File:TrailerUSHelp.jpg)

The US trailer for *[Help!](/wiki/Help!_(film) "Help! (film)")* with (from the rear) Harrison, McCartney, Lennon and (largely obscured) Starr

Controversy erupted in June 1965 when Queen [Elizabeth II](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II") appointed all four Beatles Members of the [Order of the British Empire](/wiki/Order_of_the_British_Empire "Order of the British Empire") (MBE) after Prime Minister [Harold Wilson](/wiki/Harold_Wilson "Harold Wilson") nominated them for the award.[[142]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpitz2005556-148) In protest – the honour was at that time primarily bestowed upon military veterans and civic leaders – some conservative MBE recipients returned their insignia.[[143]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpitz2005557-149)

In July, the Beatles' second film, *[Help!](/wiki/Help!_(film) "Help! (film)")*, was released, again directed by Lester. Described as "mainly a relentless spoof of [Bond](/wiki/Production_of_the_James_Bond_films "Production of the James Bond films")",[[144]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007275-150) it inspired a mixed response among both reviewers and the band. McCartney said: "*Help!* was great but it wasn't our film – we were sort of guest stars. It was fun, but basically, as an idea for a film, it was a bit wrong."[[145]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007274-151) The soundtrack was dominated by Lennon, who wrote and sang lead on most of its songs, including the two singles: "[Help!](/wiki/Help!_(song) "Help! (song)")" and "[Ticket to Ride](/wiki/Ticket_to_Ride_(song) "Ticket to Ride (song)")".[[146]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007276–277-152)

The *[Help!](/wiki/Help! "Help!")* album, the group's fifth studio LP, mirrored *A Hard Day's Night* by featuring soundtrack songs on side one and additional songs from the same sessions on side two.[[147]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn198862-153) The LP contained all original material save for two covers, "[Act Naturally](/wiki/Act_Naturally "Act Naturally")" and "[Dizzy Miss Lizzy](/wiki/Dizzy_Miss_Lizzy "Dizzy Miss Lizzy")"; they were the last covers the band would include on an album until *[Let It Be](/wiki/Let_It_Be_(album) "Let It Be (album)")*'s brief rendition of the traditional Liverpool folk song "[Maggie Mae](/wiki/Maggie_May_(folk_song) "Maggie May (folk song)")".[[148]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007276–280-154) The band expanded their use of vocal overdubs on *Help!* and incorporated classical instruments into some arrangements, including a string quartet on the pop ballad "[Yesterday](/wiki/Yesterday_(Beatles_song) "Yesterday (Beatles song)")".[[149]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007290–292Lewisohn198859,_62-155) Composed and sung by McCartney – none of the other Beatles perform on the recording[[150]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn198859-156) – "Yesterday" has inspired the most cover versions of any song ever written.[[151]](#cite_note-157) With *Help!*, the Beatles became the first rock group to be nominated for a [Grammy Award for Album of the Year](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Album_of_the_Year "Grammy Award for Album of the Year").[[152]](#cite_note-158)

[![The Beatles at a press conference in August 1965](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cd/Beatles_press_conference_1965.jpg/330px-Beatles_press_conference_1965.jpg)](/wiki/File:Beatles_press_conference_1965.jpg)

The Beatles at a press conference in [Minnesota](/wiki/Minnesota "Minnesota") in August 1965, shortly after playing at [Shea Stadium](/wiki/Shea_Stadium "Shea Stadium") in New York

The group's third US tour opened with a performance before a world-record crowd of 55,600 at New York's [Shea Stadium](/wiki/Shea_Stadium "Shea Stadium") on 15 August – "perhaps the most famous of all Beatles' concerts", in Lewisohn's description.[[153]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn1992181-159) A further nine successful concerts followed in other American cities. At a show in Atlanta, the Beatles gave one of the first live performances ever to make use of a [foldback](/wiki/Foldback_(sound_engineering) "Foldback (sound engineering)") system of on-stage monitor speakers.[[154]](#cite_note-160) Towards the end of the tour, they met with [Elvis Presley](/wiki/Elvis_Presley "Elvis Presley"), a foundational musical influence on the band, who invited them to his home in [Beverly Hills](/wiki/Beverly_Hills "Beverly Hills").[[155]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarry2000a882–883-161)[[156]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007283–284-162) Presley later said the band was an example of a trend of anti-Americanism and drug abuse.[[157]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThe_Beatles2000192-163)[[158]](#cite_note-164)

September 1965 saw the launch of an American [Saturday-morning cartoon](/wiki/Saturday-morning_cartoon "Saturday-morning cartoon") series, *[The Beatles](/wiki/The_Beatles_(TV_series) "The Beatles (TV series)")*, that echoed *A Hard Day's Night*'s slapstick antics over its two-year original run.[[159]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcNeil199682-165) The series was the first weekly television series to feature animated versions of real, living people.[[160]](#cite_note-Animators-166)

In mid-October, the Beatles entered the recording studio; for the first time when making an album, they had an extended period without other major commitments.[[161]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn1992202-167) Until this time, according to George Martin, "we had been making albums rather like a collection of singles. Now we were really beginning to think about albums as a bit of art on their own."[[162]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHertsgaard1995149–150-168) Released in December, *[Rubber Soul](/wiki/Rubber_Soul "Rubber Soul")* was hailed by critics as a major step forward in the maturity and complexity of the band's music.[[163]](#cite_note-Rubber_Soul_AllMusic-169) Their thematic reach was beginning to expand as they embraced deeper aspects of romance and philosophy, a development that NEMS executive [Peter Brown](/wiki/Peter_Brown_(music_industry) "Peter Brown (music industry)") attributed to the band members' "now habitual use of marijuana".[[164]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrownGaines2002181–182-170) Lennon referred to *Rubber Soul* as "the pot album"[[165]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThe_Beatles2000194-171) and Starr said: "Grass was really influential in a lot of our changes, especially with the writers. And because they were writing different material, we were playing differently."[[165]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThe_Beatles2000194-171) After *Help!*'s foray into classical music with flutes and strings, Harrison's introduction of a [sitar](/wiki/Sitar "Sitar") on "[Norwegian Wood (This Bird Has Flown)](/wiki/Norwegian_Wood_(This_Bird_Has_Flown) "Norwegian Wood (This Bird Has Flown)")" marked a further progression outside the traditional boundaries of popular music. As the lyrics grew more artful, fans began to study them for deeper meaning.[[166]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007297–298,_423-172)

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Gnome-mime-audio-openclipart.svg/60px-Gnome-mime-audio-openclipart.svg.png)

["Norwegian Wood (This Bird Has Flown)"](/wiki/File:Beatles_norwegian_wood.ogg "File:Beatles norwegian wood.ogg")

Sample of "Norwegian Wood" from *Rubber Soul* (1965). Harrison's use of a [sitar](/wiki/Sitar "Sitar") on this song is representative of the Beatles' incorporation of unconventional instrumentation into rock music.[[163]](#cite_note-Rubber_Soul_AllMusic-169)

---

*Problems playing this file? See [media help](/wiki/Help:Media "Help:Media").*

While some of *Rubber Soul*'s songs were the product of Lennon and McCartney's collaborative songwriting,[[167]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpitz2005584–592-173) the album also included distinct compositions from each,[[168]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiles1997268,_276,_278–279-174) though they continued to share official credit. "[In My Life](/wiki/In_My_Life "In My Life")", of which each later claimed lead authorship, is considered a highlight of the entire Lennon–McCartney catalogue.[[169]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpitz2005587-175) Harrison called *Rubber Soul* his "favourite album",[[165]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThe_Beatles2000194-171) and Starr referred to it as "the departure record".[[170]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpitz2005591-176) McCartney has said, "We'd had our cute period, and now it was time to expand."[[171]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThe_Beatles2000197-177) However, recording engineer [Norman Smith](/wiki/Norman_Smith_(record_producer) "Norman Smith (record producer)") later stated that the studio sessions revealed signs of growing conflict within the group – "the clash between John and Paul was becoming obvious", he wrote, and "as far as Paul was concerned, George could do no right".[[172]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarry2000b780-178)

In an interview with [Dan Rather](/wiki/Dan_Rather "Dan Rather") which aired on [AXS TV](/wiki/AXS_TV "AXS TV") on 10 May 2024, Starr said that "we didn't get along, we were four guys, we had rows," noting that their relationship was getting more tense even by the time he had children.[[57]](#cite_note-starrrathermay2024-58) Nevertheless, according to Starr, the band lasted as long as it did due to their music ambitions.[[57]](#cite_note-starrrathermay2024-58)

#### Controversies, *Revolver* and final tour

Capitol Records, from December 1963 when it began issuing Beatles recordings for the US market, exercised complete control over format,[[90]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn1992350-92) compiling distinct US albums from the band's recordings and issuing songs of their choosing as singles.[[173]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007295–296-179)[[nb 7]](#cite_note-181) In June 1966, the Capitol LP *[Yesterday and Today](/wiki/Yesterday_and_Today "Yesterday and Today")* caused an uproar with its cover, which portrayed the grinning Beatles dressed in butcher's overalls, accompanied by raw meat and mutilated plastic baby dolls. According to Beatles biographer [Bill Harry](/wiki/Bill_Harry "Bill Harry"), it has been incorrectly suggested that this was meant as a satirical response to the way Capitol had "butchered" the US versions of the band's albums.[[175]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarry2000a1187-182) Thousands of copies of the LP had a new cover pasted over the original; an unpeeled "first-state" copy fetched $10,500 at a December 2005 auction.[[176]](#cite_note-183) In England, meanwhile, Harrison met sitar maestro [Ravi Shankar](/wiki/Ravi_Shankar "Ravi Shankar"), who agreed to train him on the instrument.[[177]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELavezzoli2006176-184)

The Beatles toured Japan the month after the *Yesterday and Today* furore, facing death threats from Japanese Conservatives due to the Beatles unknowingly performing at the [Budokan Stadium](/wiki/Nippon_Budokan "Nippon Budokan"), which was designated as a place to revere Japan's war dead. When they toured the Philippines a few days later, the Beatles unintentionally snubbed the nation's first lady, [Imelda Marcos](/wiki/Imelda_Marcos "Imelda Marcos"), who had expected them to attend a breakfast reception at the [Presidential Palace](/wiki/Malaca%C3%B1ang_Palace "Malacañang Palace").[[178]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpitz2005619-185) When presented with the invitation, Epstein politely declined on the band members' behalf, as it had never been his policy to accept such official invitations.[[179]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpitz2005620-186) They soon found that the [Marcos regime](/wiki/Marcos_regime "Marcos regime") was unaccustomed to taking no for an answer. The resulting riots endangered the group and they escaped the country with difficulty.[[180]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpitz2005623-187) Immediately afterwards, the band members visited India for the first time.[[181]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELavezzoli2006177-188)

> We're [more popular than Jesus](/wiki/More_popular_than_Jesus "More popular than Jesus") now; I don't know which will go first – rock 'n' roll or Christianity.

– John Lennon, 1966[[182]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007309-189)

Almost as soon as they returned home, the Beatles faced a fierce backlash from US religious and social conservatives (as well as the [Ku Klux Klan](/wiki/Ku_Klux_Klan "Ku Klux Klan")) over a comment Lennon had made in a March interview with British reporter [Maureen Cleave](/wiki/Maureen_Cleave "Maureen Cleave").[[183]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn1992212–213-190) "Christianity will go", Lennon had said. "It will vanish and shrink. I needn't argue about that; I'm right and I will be proved right ... [Jesus](/wiki/Jesus "Jesus") was alright but his [disciples](/wiki/Disciples_(Christianity) "Disciples (Christianity)") were thick and ordinary. It's them twisting it that ruins it for me."[[184]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007307–309-191) His comments went virtually unnoticed in England, but when US teenage fan magazine *Datebook* printed them five months later, it sparked a controversy with Christians in America's conservative [Bible Belt](/wiki/Bible_Belt "Bible Belt") region.[[183]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn1992212–213-190) The [Vatican](/wiki/Holy_See "Holy See") issued a protest, and bans on Beatles records were imposed by Spanish and Dutch stations and South Africa's [national broadcasting service](/wiki/South_African_Broadcasting_Corporation "South African Broadcasting Corporation").[[185]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENorman2008449-192) Epstein accused *Datebook* of having taken Lennon's words out of context. At a press conference, Lennon pointed out, "If I'd said television was more popular than Jesus, I might have got away with it."[[186]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007346-193) He claimed that he was referring to how other people viewed their success, but at the prompting of reporters, he concluded: "If you want me to apologise, if that will make you happy, then okay, I'm sorry."[[186]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007346-193)

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Gnome-mime-audio-openclipart.svg/60px-Gnome-mime-audio-openclipart.svg.png)

["Eleanor Rigby"](/wiki/File:Beatles_eleanor_rigby.ogg "File:Beatles eleanor rigby.ogg")

Sample of "[Eleanor Rigby](/wiki/Eleanor_Rigby "Eleanor Rigby")" from *Revolver* (1966). The album involves innovative compositional approaches, arrangements and recording techniques. This song, primarily written by McCartney, prominently features classical strings in a novel fusion of musical styles.

---

*Problems playing this file? See [media help](/wiki/Help:Media "Help:Media").*

Released in August 1966, a week before the Beatles' final tour, *[Revolver](/wiki/Revolver_(Beatles_album) "Revolver (Beatles album)")* marked another artistic step forward for the group.[[187]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007348-194) The album featured sophisticated songwriting, studio experimentation and a greatly expanded repertoire of musical styles, ranging from innovative classical string arrangements to [psychedelia](/wiki/Psychedelic_music "Psychedelic music").[[187]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007348-194) Abandoning the customary group photograph, its [Aubrey Beardsley](/wiki/Aubrey_Beardsley "Aubrey Beardsley")-inspired cover – designed by [Klaus Voormann](/wiki/Klaus_Voormann "Klaus Voormann"), a friend of the band since their Hamburg days – was a monochrome collage and line drawing caricature of the group.[[187]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007348-194) The album was preceded by the single "[Paperback Writer](/wiki/Paperback_Writer "Paperback Writer")", backed by "[Rain](/wiki/Rain_(Beatles_song) "Rain (Beatles song)")".[[188]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn1992350–351-195) Short promotional films were made for both songs; described by cultural historian Saul Austerlitz as "among the first true music videos",[[189]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAusterlitz200718-196) they aired on *The Ed Sullivan Show* and *[Top of the Pops](/wiki/Top_of_the_Pops "Top of the Pops")* in June.[[190]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn1992221–222-197)

Among the experimental songs on *Revolver* was "[Tomorrow Never Knows](/wiki/Tomorrow_Never_Knows "Tomorrow Never Knows")", the lyrics for which Lennon drew from [Timothy Leary](/wiki/Timothy_Leary "Timothy Leary")'s *[The Psychedelic Experience: A Manual Based on the Tibetan Book of the Dead](/wiki/The_Psychedelic_Experience:_A_Manual_Based_on_the_Tibetan_Book_of_the_Dead "The Psychedelic Experience: A Manual Based on the Tibetan Book of the Dead")*. Its creation involved eight tape decks distributed about the EMI building, each staffed by an engineer or band member, who randomly varied the movement of a [tape loop](/wiki/Tape_loop "Tape loop") while Martin created a composite recording by sampling the incoming data.[[191]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007364–366-198) McCartney's "[Eleanor Rigby](/wiki/Eleanor_Rigby "Eleanor Rigby")" made prominent use of a [string octet](/wiki/Octet_(music) "Octet (music)"); Gould describes it as "a true hybrid, conforming to no recognisable style or genre of song".[[192]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007350,_402-199) Harrison's emergence as a songwriter was reflected in three of his compositions appearing on the record.[[193]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchaffner197863-200) Among these, "[Taxman](/wiki/Taxman "Taxman")", which opened the album, marked the first example of the Beatles making a political statement through their music.[[194]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETurner2016162-201)

[![San Francisco's Candlestick Park in the 1960s](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/Candlestick_Postcard_-_01.JPG/330px-Candlestick_Postcard_-_01.JPG)](/wiki/File:Candlestick_Postcard_-_01.JPG)

San Francisco's [Candlestick Park](/wiki/Candlestick_Park "Candlestick Park") (pictured in the early 1960s) was the venue for the Beatles' final concert before a paying audience.

As preparations were made for a tour of the US, the Beatles knew that their music would hardly be heard. Having originally used [Vox AC30](/wiki/Vox_AC30 "Vox AC30") amplifiers, they later acquired more powerful 100-watt amplifiers, specially designed for them by [Vox](/wiki/Vox_(musical_equipment) "Vox (musical equipment)"), as they moved into larger venues in 1964; however, these were still inadequate. Struggling to compete with the volume of sound generated by screaming fans, the band had grown increasingly bored with the routine of performing live.[[195]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarry2000a1093-202) Recognising that their shows were no longer about the music, and shaken up from the recent incident in the Philippines, they decided to make the August tour their last.[[196]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn1992210,_230-203)[[197]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpitz2005629-204)

The band performed none of their new songs on the tour.[[198]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn1992361–365-205) In Chris Ingham's description, they were very much "studio creations ... and there was no way a four-piece rock 'n' roll group could do them justice, particularly through the desensitising wall of the fans' screams. 'Live Beatles' and 'Studio Beatles' had become entirely different beasts."[[199]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIngham200644-206) The band's concert at San Francisco's [Candlestick Park](/wiki/Candlestick_Park "Candlestick Park") on 29 August was their last commercial concert.[[200]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiles1997293–295-207) It marked the end of four years dominated by almost nonstop touring that included over 1,400 concert appearances internationally.[[201]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould20075–6,_249,_281,_347-208)

### 1966–1970: Studio years

#### *Sgt. Pepper's Lonely Hearts Club Band*

Main article: [*Sgt. Pepper's Lonely Hearts Club Band*](/wiki/Sgt._Pepper%27s_Lonely_Hearts_Club_Band "Sgt. Pepper's Lonely Hearts Club Band")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/Sgt._Pepper%27s_Lonely_Hearts_Club_Band_album_art.jpg/250px-Sgt._Pepper%27s_Lonely_Hearts_Club_Band_album_art.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sgt._Pepper%27s_Lonely_Hearts_Club_Band_album_art.jpg)

Front cover of *[Sgt. Pepper's Lonely Hearts Club Band](/wiki/Sgt._Pepper%27s_Lonely_Hearts_Club_Band "Sgt. Pepper's Lonely Hearts Club Band")*, "the most famous cover of any music album, and one of the most imitated images in the world"[[202]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarry2000a970-209)

Freed from the burden of touring, the Beatles embraced an increasingly experimental approach as they recorded *[Sgt. Pepper's Lonely Hearts Club Band](/wiki/Sgt._Pepper%27s_Lonely_Hearts_Club_Band "Sgt. Pepper's Lonely Hearts Club Band")*, beginning in late November 1966.[[203]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn1992232-210) According to engineer [Geoff Emerick](/wiki/Geoff_Emerick "Geoff Emerick"), the album's recording took over 700 hours.[[204]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEmerickMassey2006190-211) He recalled the band's insistence "that everything on *Sgt. Pepper* had to be different. We had microphones right down in the bells of brass instruments and headphones turned into microphones attached to violins. We used giant primitive oscillators to vary the speed of instruments and vocals and we had tapes chopped to pieces and stuck together upside down and the wrong way around." Parts of "[A Day in the Life](/wiki/A_Day_in_the_Life "A Day in the Life")" featured a 40-piece orchestra.[[205]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007387–388-212) The sessions initially yielded the non-album [double A-side](/wiki/Double_A-side "Double A-side") single "[Strawberry Fields Forever](/wiki/Strawberry_Fields_Forever "Strawberry Fields Forever")"/"[Penny Lane](/wiki/Penny_Lane "Penny Lane")" in February 1967;[[206]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacDonald2005221-213) the *Sgt. Pepper* LP followed with a rush-release in May.[[207]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEverett1999123-214) The musical complexity of the records, created using relatively primitive [four-track](/wiki/Multitrack_recording "Multitrack recording") recording technology, astounded contemporary artists.[[202]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarry2000a970-209) Among music critics, acclaim for the album was virtually universal.[[208]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007420–425-215) Gould writes:

> The overwhelming consensus is that the Beatles had created a popular masterpiece: a rich, sustained, and overflowing work of collaborative genius whose bold ambition and startling originality dramatically enlarged the possibilities and raised the expectations of what the experience of listening to popular music on record could be. On the basis of this perception, *Sgt. Pepper* became the catalyst for an explosion of mass enthusiasm for album-formatted rock that would revolutionise both the aesthetics and the economics of the record business in ways that far outstripped the earlier pop explosions triggered by the Elvis phenomenon of 1956 and the Beatlemania phenomenon of 1963.[[209]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007418-216)

In the wake of *Sgt. Pepper*, the underground and mainstream press widely publicised the Beatles as leaders of youth culture, as well as "lifestyle revolutionaries".[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFrontani2007157-4) The album was the first major pop/rock LP to include its complete lyrics, which appeared on the back cover.[[210]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn1992236-217)[[211]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEInglis200896-218) Those lyrics were the subject of critical analysis; for instance, in late 1967 the album was the subject of a scholarly inquiry by American literary critic and professor of English [Richard Poirier](/wiki/Richard_Poirier "Richard Poirier"), who observed that his students were "listening to the group's music with a degree of engagement that he, as a teacher of literature, could only envy".[[212]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007423–425-219)[[nb 8]](#cite_note-220) The elaborate cover also attracted considerable interest and study.[[213]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007394–395-221) A collage designed by [pop artists](/wiki/Pop_art "Pop art") [Peter Blake](/wiki/Peter_Blake_(artist) "Peter Blake (artist)") and [Jann Haworth](/wiki/Jann_Haworth "Jann Haworth"), it depicted the group as the fictional band referred to in the album's [title track](/wiki/Sgt._Pepper%27s_Lonely_Hearts_Club_Band_(song) "Sgt. Pepper's Lonely Hearts Club Band (song)")[[214]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacDonald2005312-222) standing in front of [a crowd of famous people](/wiki/List_of_images_on_the_cover_of_Sgt._Pepper%27s_Lonely_Hearts_Club_Band "List of images on the cover of Sgt. Pepper's Lonely Hearts Club Band").[[215]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThe_Beatles2000248-223) The heavy moustaches worn by the group reflected the growing influence of the [hippie](/wiki/Hippie "Hippie") movement,[[216]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThe_Beatles2000236-224) while cultural historian Jonathan Harris describes their "brightly coloured parodies of military uniforms" as a knowingly "anti-authoritarian and anti-establishment" display.[[217]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarris200512–13-225)

*Sgt. Pepper* topped the UK charts for 23 consecutive weeks, with a further four weeks at number one until February 1968.[[218]](#cite_note-226) With 2.5 million copies sold within three months of its release,[[219]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFrontani2007147-227) *Sgt. Pepper*'s initial commercial success exceeded that of all previous Beatles albums.[[220]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpitz2005697-228) It was the first rock album to win the [Grammy Award for Album of the Year](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Album_of_the_Year "Grammy Award for Album of the Year").[[221]](#cite_note-229) It sustained its immense popularity into the 21st century while breaking numerous sales records.[[222]](#cite_note-230)

#### *Magical Mystery Tour* and *Yellow Submarine*

Main articles: [*Magical Mystery Tour* (film)](/wiki/Magical_Mystery_Tour_(film) "Magical Mystery Tour (film)") and [*Yellow Submarine* (film)](/wiki/Yellow_Submarine_(film) "Yellow Submarine (film)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/The_Beatles_1967.jpg/250px-The_Beatles_1967.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Beatles_1967.jpg)

The Beatles in a promotional photo for "[All You Need Is Love](/wiki/All_You_Need_Is_Love "All You Need Is Love")" in 1967

Two Beatles film projects were conceived within weeks of completing *Sgt.
