Pepper*: *[Magical Mystery Tour](/wiki/Magical_Mystery_Tour_(film) "Magical Mystery Tour (film)")*, a one-hour television film, and *[Yellow Submarine](/wiki/Yellow_Submarine_(film) "Yellow Submarine (film)")*, an animated feature-length film produced by [United Artists](/wiki/United_Artists "United Artists").[[223]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn198841,_110–111,_122-231) The group began recording music for the former in late April 1967, but the project then lay dormant as they focused on recording songs for the latter.[[224]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiles2001276–77-232) On 25 June, the Beatles performed their forthcoming single "[All You Need Is Love](/wiki/All_You_Need_Is_Love "All You Need Is Love")" to an estimated 350 million viewers on *[Our World](/wiki/Our_World_(1967_TV_program) "Our World (1967 TV program)")*, the first live global television link.[[225]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn1992237,_259–260-233) Released a week later, during the [Summer of Love](/wiki/Summer_of_Love "Summer of Love"), the song was adopted as a [flower power](/wiki/Flower_power "Flower power") anthem.[[226]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007428–429-234) The Beatles' use of psychedelic drugs was at its height during that summer.[[227]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEverett1999129-235) In July and August, the group pursued interests related to similar utopian-based ideology, including a week-long investigation into the possibility of starting an island-based [commune](/wiki/Intentional_community "Intentional community") off the coast of Greece.[[228]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWomack2007197-236)[[229]](#cite_note-237)

On 24 August, the group were introduced to [Maharishi Mahesh Yogi](/wiki/Maharishi_Mahesh_Yogi "Maharishi Mahesh Yogi") in London. The next day, they travelled to [Bangor](/wiki/Bangor,_Gwynedd "Bangor, Gwynedd") for his [Transcendental Meditation](/wiki/Transcendental_Meditation "Transcendental Meditation") retreat. On 27 August, their manager's assistant, Peter Brown, phoned to inform them that Epstein had died.[[230]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpitz2005709,_713–719-238) The coroner ruled the death an accidental [carbitol](/wiki/Bromoureide "Bromoureide") overdose, although it was widely rumoured to be a suicide.[[231]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrownGaines2002249-239)[[nb 9]](#cite_note-241) His death left the group disoriented and fearful about the future.[[233]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThe_Beatles2000268-242) Lennon recalled: "We collapsed. I knew that we were in trouble then. I didn't really have any misconceptions about our ability to do anything other than play music, and I was scared. I thought, 'We've fuckin' had it now.'"[[234]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENorman2008508-243) Harrison's then-wife [Pattie Boyd](/wiki/Pattie_Boyd "Pattie Boyd") remembered that "Paul and George were in complete shock. I don't think it could have been worse if they had heard that their own fathers had dropped dead."[[235]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBoyd2008106–107-244) During a band meeting in September, McCartney recommended that the band proceed with *Magical Mystery Tour*.[[224]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiles2001276–77-232)

The [*Magical Mystery Tour* soundtrack](/wiki/Magical_Mystery_Tour "Magical Mystery Tour") was released in the UK as a six-track double [extended play](/wiki/Extended_play "Extended play") (EP) in early December 1967.[[90]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn1992350-92)[[236]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007452-245) It was the first example of a double EP in the UK.[[237]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeaverson199753-246)[[238]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELarkin2006488-247) The record carried on the psychedelic vein of *Sgt. Pepper*,[[239]](#cite_note-248) though in line with the band's wishes, the packaging reinforced the idea that the release was a film soundtrack rather than a follow-up to *Sgt. Pepper*.[[236]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007452-245) In the US, the soundtrack appeared as an identically titled LP that also included five tracks from the band's recent singles.[[115]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn1992351-118) In its first three weeks, the album set a record for the highest initial sales of any Capitol LP and is the only Capitol compilation later to be adopted in the band's official canon of studio albums.[[240]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarry2000a699-249)

*Magical Mystery Tour* first aired on [Boxing Day](/wiki/Boxing_Day "Boxing Day") to an audience of approximately 15 million.[[241]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchaffner197890-250) Largely directed by McCartney, the film was the band's first critical failure in the UK.[[242]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiles1997368–69-251) It was dismissed as "blatant rubbish" by the *[Daily Express](/wiki/Daily_Express "Daily Express")*, the *[Daily Mail](/wiki/Daily_Mail "Daily Mail")* called it "a colossal conceit" and *[The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")* labelled the film "a kind of fantasy morality play about the grossness and warmth and stupidity of the audience".[[243]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007455–456-252) Gould describes it as "a great deal of raw footage showing a group of people getting on, getting off, and riding on a bus".[[243]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007455–456-252) Although the viewership figures were respectable, its slating in the press led US television networks to lose interest in broadcasting the film.[[244]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarry2000a703-253)

The group were less involved with *Yellow Submarine*, which featured the band appearing as themselves for only a short live-action segment.[[245]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn1992276-254) Premiering in July 1968, the film featured cartoon versions of the band members and a soundtrack with eleven of their songs, including four unreleased studio recordings that made their debut in the film.[[246]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007485-255) Critics praised the film for its music, humour and innovative visual style.[[247]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007487–88,_505–506-256) A [soundtrack LP](/wiki/Yellow_Submarine_(album) "Yellow Submarine (album)") was issued seven months later; it contained those four new songs, the title track (already issued on *Revolver*), "All You Need Is Love" (already issued as a single and on the US *Magical Mystery Tour* LP) and seven instrumental pieces composed by Martin.[[248]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn1992304,_350-257)

#### India retreat, Apple Corps and the White Album

Main articles: [Beatles in India](/wiki/Beatles_in_India "Beatles in India"), [Apple Corps](/wiki/Apple_Corps "Apple Corps"), and [*The Beatles* (album)](/wiki/The_Beatles_(album) "The Beatles (album)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/The_Beatles_1968_press_photo.jpg/250px-The_Beatles_1968_press_photo.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Beatles_1968_press_photo.jpg)

The Beatles pictured in February 1968

In February 1968, the Beatles travelled to Maharishi Mahesh Yogi's [ashram](/wiki/Ashram "Ashram") in [Rishikesh](/wiki/Rishikesh "Rishikesh"), India, to take part in a three-month meditation "Guide Course". Their [time in India](/wiki/The_Beatles_in_India "The Beatles in India") marked one of the band's most prolific periods, yielding numerous songs, including a majority of those on their next album.[[249]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarry2000a108–109-258) However, Starr left after only ten days, unable to stomach the food, and McCartney eventually grew bored and departed a month later.[[250]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007463–468-259) For Lennon and Harrison, creativity turned to question when an electronics technician known as [Magic Alex](/wiki/Magic_Alex "Magic Alex") suggested that the Maharishi was attempting to manipulate them.[[251]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarry2000a705–706-260) When he alleged that the Maharishi had made sexual advances to women attendees, a persuaded Lennon left abruptly just two months into the course, bringing an unconvinced Harrison and the remainder of the group's entourage with him.[[250]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007463–468-259) In anger, Lennon wrote a scathing song titled "Maharishi", renamed "[Sexy Sadie](/wiki/Sexy_Sadie "Sexy Sadie")" to avoid potential legal issues. McCartney said, "We made a mistake. We thought there was more to him than there was."[[251]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarry2000a705–706-260)

In May, Lennon and McCartney travelled to New York for the public unveiling of the Beatles' new business venture, [Apple Corps](/wiki/Apple_Corps "Apple Corps").[[252]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn1992282-261) It was initially formed several months earlier as part of a plan to create a tax-effective business structure, but the band then desired to extend the corporation to other pursuits, including record distribution, peace activism and education.[[253]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDoggett201120,_26-262) McCartney described Apple as "rather like a Western communism".[[254]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDoggett201126-263) The enterprise drained the group financially with a series of unsuccessful projects[[255]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacDonald2005280–281-264) handled largely by members of the Beatles' entourage, who were given their jobs regardless of talent and experience.[[256]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDoggett201122-265) Among its numerous subsidiaries were [Apple Electronics](/wiki/Apple_Electronics "Apple Electronics"), established to foster technological innovations with Magic Alex at the head, and Apple Retailing, which opened the short-lived [Apple Boutique](/wiki/Apple_Boutique "Apple Boutique") in London.[[257]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDoggett201120,_22,_25,_35-266) Harrison later said, "Basically, it was chaos ... John and Paul got carried away with the idea and blew millions, and Ringo and I just had to go along with it."[[254]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDoggett201126-263)

[![The album artwork of the Beatles' self-titled 1968 album, also known as "the White Album"](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/20/TheBeatles68LP.jpg/250px-TheBeatles68LP.jpg)](/wiki/File:TheBeatles68LP.jpg)

*The Beatles*, known as "the White Album" for its minimalist cover, conceived by pop artist [Richard Hamilton](/wiki/Richard_Hamilton_(artist) "Richard Hamilton (artist)") "in direct contrast to *Sgt. Pepper*", while also suggesting a "clean slate"[[258]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007510–511-267)

From late May to mid-October 1968, the group recorded what became *[The Beatles](/wiki/The_Beatles_(album) "The Beatles (album)")*, a double LP commonly known as "the White Album" for its virtually featureless cover.[[259]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007510-268) During this time, relations between the members grew openly divisive.[[260]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacDonald2005310-269) Starr quit for two weeks, leaving his bandmates to record "[Back in the U.S.S.R.](/wiki/Back_in_the_U.S.S.R. "Back in the U.S.S.R.")" and "[Dear Prudence](/wiki/Dear_Prudence "Dear Prudence")" as a trio, with McCartney filling in on drums.[[261]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWinn2009205–207-270) Lennon had lost interest in collaborating with McCartney,[[262]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007513,_516-271) whose contribution "[Ob-La-Di, Ob-La-Da](/wiki/Ob-La-Di,_Ob-La-Da "Ob-La-Di, Ob-La-Da")" he scorned as "granny music shit".[[263]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEmerickMassey2006246-272) Tensions were further aggravated by Lennon's romantic preoccupation with [avant-garde](/wiki/Avant-garde "Avant-garde") artist [Yoko Ono](/wiki/Yoko_Ono "Yoko Ono"), whom he insisted on bringing to the sessions despite the group's well-established understanding that girlfriends were not allowed in the studio.[[264]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarry2000b103-273) McCartney has recalled that the album "wasn't a pleasant one to make".[[265]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThe_Beatles2000310-274) He and Lennon identified the sessions as the start of the band's break-up.[[266]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThe_Beatles2000237-275)[[267]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarry2000b102-276)

With the record, the band executed a wider range of musical styles[[268]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiles2001315-277) and broke with their recent tradition of incorporating several musical styles in one song by keeping each piece of music consistently faithful to a select genre.[[269]](#cite_note-278) During the sessions, the group upgraded to an eight-track tape console, which made it easier for them to layer tracks piecemeal, while the members often recorded independently of each other, affording the album a reputation as a collection of solo recordings rather than a unified group effort.[[270]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn1988137,_146,_150,_152-279) Describing the double album, Lennon later said: "Every track is an individual track; there isn't any Beatle music on it. [It's] John and the band, Paul and the band, George and the band."[[271]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007509-280) The sessions also produced the Beatles' longest song yet, "[Hey Jude](/wiki/Hey_Jude "Hey Jude")", released in August as a non-album single with "[Revolution](/wiki/Revolution_(Beatles_song) "Revolution (Beatles song)")".[[272]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn1988152-281)

Issued in November, the White Album was the band's first [Apple Records](/wiki/Apple_Records "Apple Records") album release, although EMI continued to own their recordings.[[273]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn1992278-282) The record attracted more than 2 million advance orders, selling nearly 4 million copies in the US in little over a month, and its tracks dominated the playlists of American radio stations.[[274]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007528-283) Its lyrical content was the focus of much analysis by the counterculture.[[275]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacDonald2005311–313-284) Despite its popularity, reviewers were largely confused by the album's content and it failed to inspire the level of critical writing that *Sgt. Pepper* had.[[274]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007528-283)

#### *Abbey Road*, *Let It Be* and separation

See also: [Break-up of the Beatles](/wiki/Break-up_of_the_Beatles "Break-up of the Beatles")

Although *[Let It Be](/wiki/Let_It_Be_(album) "Let It Be (album)")* was the Beatles' final album release, it was largely recorded before *[Abbey Road](/wiki/Abbey_Road "Abbey Road")*. The project's impetus came from an idea Martin attributes to McCartney, who suggested they "record an album of new material and rehearse it, then perform it before a live audience for the very first time – on record and on film".[[276]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarry2000b539-285) Originally intended for a one-hour television programme to be called *Beatles at Work*, in the event much of the album's content came from studio work beginning in January 1969, many hours of which were captured on film by director [Michael Lindsay-Hogg](/wiki/Michael_Lindsay-Hogg "Michael Lindsay-Hogg").[[276]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarry2000b539-285)[[277]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn1992306–307-286) Martin said that the project was "not at all a happy recording experience. It was a time when relations between the Beatles were at their lowest ebb."[[276]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarry2000b539-285) Lennon described the largely impromptu sessions as "hell ... the most miserable ... on Earth", and Harrison, "the low of all-time".[[278]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn1992310-287) Irritated by McCartney and Lennon, Harrison walked out for five days. Upon returning, he threatened to leave the band unless they "abandon[ed] all talk of live performance" and instead focused on finishing a new album, initially titled *Get Back*, using songs recorded for the TV special.[[279]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn1992307-288) He also demanded they cease work at [Twickenham Film Studios](/wiki/Twickenham_Film_Studios "Twickenham Film Studios"), where the sessions had begun, and relocate to the newly finished [Apple Studio](/wiki/Apple_Studio "Apple Studio"). His bandmates agreed and it was decided to salvage the footage shot for the TV production for use in a feature film.[[280]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn1992306–307,_309-289)

[![American musician Billy Preston in 1971](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/Billy_Preston_perforning_in_1971.jpg/250px-Billy_Preston_perforning_in_1971.jpg)](/wiki/File:Billy_Preston_perforning_in_1971.jpg)

The American soul musician [Billy Preston](/wiki/Billy_Preston "Billy Preston") (pictured in 1971) was, for a short time, considered a [fifth Beatle](/wiki/Fifth_Beatle "Fifth Beatle") during the *Get Back* sessions.

To alleviate tensions within the band and improve the quality of their live sound, Harrison invited keyboardist [Billy Preston](/wiki/Billy_Preston "Billy Preston") to participate in the last nine days of sessions.[[281]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn1992309–314-290) Preston received label billing on the "[Get Back](/wiki/Get_Back "Get Back")" single – the only musician ever to receive that acknowledgment on an official Beatles release.[[282]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarry2000a451,_660-291) After the rehearsals, the band could not agree on a location to film a concert, rejecting several ideas, including a boat at sea, a lunatic asylum, the Libyan desert and the [Colosseum](/wiki/Colosseum "Colosseum").[[276]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarry2000b539-285) Ultimately, what would be their [final live performance](/wiki/The_Beatles%27_rooftop_concert "The Beatles' rooftop concert") was filmed on the rooftop of the Apple Corps building at 3 [Savile Row](/wiki/Savile_Row "Savile Row"), London, on 30 January 1969.[[283]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn1992307–308,_312-292) Five weeks later, engineer [Glyn Johns](/wiki/Glyn_Johns "Glyn Johns"), whom Lewisohn describes as *Get Back*'s "uncredited producer", began work assembling an album, given "free rein" as the band "all but washed their hands of the entire project".[[284]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn1992309,_316–323-293)

[![A terrace house with four floors and an attic. It is red brick, with a slate roof, and the ground floor rendered in imitation of stone and painted white. Each upper floor has four sash windows, divided into small panes. The door, with a canopy over it, occupies the place of the second window from the left on the ground floor.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/25/3_Savile_Row.jpg/250px-3_Savile_Row.jpg)](/wiki/File:3_Savile_Row.jpg)

Apple Corps building at 3 Savile Row, site of the [*Let It Be* rooftop concert](/wiki/The_Beatles%27_rooftop_concert "The Beatles' rooftop concert")

New strains developed between the band members regarding the appointment of a financial adviser, the need for which had become evident without Epstein to manage business affairs. Lennon, Harrison and Starr favoured [Allen Klein](/wiki/Allen_Klein "Allen Klein"), who had managed [the Rolling Stones](/wiki/The_Rolling_Stones "The Rolling Stones") and [Sam Cooke](/wiki/Sam_Cooke "Sam Cooke");[[285]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarry2000a612-294) McCartney wanted [Lee](/wiki/Lee_Eastman "Lee Eastman") and John Eastman – father and brother, respectively, of [Linda Eastman](/wiki/Linda_McCartney "Linda McCartney"),[[286]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDoggett201170,_132-295) whom McCartney married on 12 March.[[287]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiles2001336-296) Agreement could not be reached, so both Klein and the Eastmans were temporarily appointed: Klein as the Beatles' business manager and the Eastmans as their lawyers.[[288]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDoggett201171–72-297)[[289]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoodman2015164–166-298) Further conflict ensued, however, and financial opportunities were lost.[[285]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarry2000a612-294) On 8 May, Klein was named sole manager of the band,[[290]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn1992322-299) the Eastmans having previously been dismissed as the Beatles' lawyers. McCartney refused to sign the management contract with Klein, but he was out-voted by the other Beatles.[[291]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoodman2015174–175-300)

Martin stated that he was surprised when McCartney asked him to produce another album, as the *Get Back* sessions had been "a miserable experience" and he had "thought it was the end of the road for all of us".[[292]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007560-301) The primary recording sessions for *Abbey Road* began on 2 July.[[293]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn1992324-302) Lennon, who rejected Martin's proposed format of a "continuously moving piece of music", wanted his and McCartney's songs to occupy separate sides of the album.[[294]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007563-303) The eventual format, with individually composed songs on the first side and the second consisting largely of a [medley](/wiki/List_of_musical_medleys "List of musical medleys"), was McCartney's suggested compromise.[[294]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007563-303) Emerick noted that the replacement of the studio's [valve](/wiki/Vacuum_tube "Vacuum tube")-based mixing console with a transistorised one yielded a less punchy sound, leaving the group frustrated at the thinner tone and lack of impact and contributing to its "kinder, gentler" feel relative to their previous albums.[[295]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEmerickMassey2006277–278-304)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a4/The_Beatles_Abbey_Road_album_cover.jpg/250px-The_Beatles_Abbey_Road_album_cover.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Beatles_Abbey_Road_album_cover.jpg)

The photo session for the *[Abbey Road](/wiki/Abbey_Road "Abbey Road")* album cover, on 8 August 1969, turned out to be the band's penultimate one. From left: Harrison, McCartney, Starr, Lennon

On 4 July, the first solo single by a Beatle was released: Lennon's "[Give Peace a Chance](/wiki/Give_Peace_a_Chance "Give Peace a Chance")", credited to the [Plastic Ono Band](/wiki/Plastic_Ono_Band "Plastic Ono Band"). The completion and mixing of "[I Want You (She's So Heavy)](/wiki/I_Want_You_(She%27s_So_Heavy) "I Want You (She's So Heavy)")" on 20 August was the last occasion on which all four Beatles were together in the same studio.[[296]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn1988191-305) On 8 September, while Starr was in hospital, the other band members met to discuss recording a new album. They considered a different approach to songwriting by ending the Lennon–McCartney pretence and having four compositions apiece from Lennon, McCartney and Harrison, with two from Starr and a lead single around Christmas.[[297]](#cite_note-8sep69-306) On 20 September, Lennon announced his departure to the rest of the group but agreed to withhold a public announcement to avoid undermining sales of the forthcoming album.[[298]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENorman2008622–624-307)

Released on 26 September, *Abbey Road* sold four million copies within three months and topped the UK charts for a total of seventeen weeks.[[299]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007593-308) Its second track, the ballad "[Something](/wiki/Something_(Beatles_song) "Something (Beatles song)")", was issued as a single – the only Harrison composition that appeared as a Beatles A-side.[[300]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiles1997553-309) *Abbey Road* received mixed reviews, although the medley met with general acclaim.[[299]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007593-308) Unterberger considers it "a fitting swan song for the group", containing "some of the greatest harmonies to be heard on any rock record".[[301]](#cite_note-310) [Musicologist](/wiki/Musicologist "Musicologist") and author [Ian MacDonald](/wiki/Ian_MacDonald "Ian MacDonald") calls the album "erratic and often hollow", despite the "semblance of unity and coherence" offered by the medley.[[302]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacDonald2005367-311) Martin singled it out as his favourite Beatles album; Lennon said it was "competent" but had "no life in it".[[295]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEmerickMassey2006277–278-304)

For the still unfinished *Get Back* album, one last song, Harrison's "[I Me Mine](/wiki/I_Me_Mine "I Me Mine")", was recorded on 3 January 1970. Lennon, in Denmark at the time, did not participate.[[303]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn1992342-312) In March, rejecting the work Johns had done on the project, now retitled *Let It Be*, Klein gave the session tapes to American producer [Phil Spector](/wiki/Phil_Spector "Phil Spector"), who had recently produced Lennon's solo single "[Instant Karma!](/wiki/Instant_Karma! "Instant Karma!")"[[304]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn1992342–343-313) In addition to remixing the material, Spector edited, spliced and overdubbed several of the recordings that had been intended as "live". McCartney was unhappy with the producer's approach and particularly dissatisfied with the lavish orchestration on "[The Long and Winding Road](/wiki/The_Long_and_Winding_Road "The Long and Winding Road")", which involved a fourteen-voice choir and 36-piece instrumental ensemble.[[305]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn1992349-314) McCartney's demands that the alterations to the song be reverted were ignored,[[306]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarry2000a682-315) and he publicly announced his departure from the band on 10 April, a week before the release of his first [self-titled solo album](/wiki/McCartney_(album) "McCartney (album)").[[305]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn1992349-314)[[307]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpitz2005853-316)

On 8 May 1970, *Let It Be* was released. Its accompanying single, "The Long and Winding Road", was expected to be the Beatles' last; it was released in the US, but not in the UK.[[188]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn1992350–351-195) The *[Let It Be](/wiki/Let_It_Be_(1970_film) "Let It Be (1970 film)")* documentary film followed later that month and would win the 1970 Academy Award for [Best Original Song Score](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Original_Score#1970s "Academy Award for Best Original Score").[[308]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESouthallPerry200696-317) *[Sunday Telegraph](/wiki/Sunday_Telegraph "Sunday Telegraph")* critic [Penelope Gilliatt](/wiki/Penelope_Gilliatt "Penelope Gilliatt") called it "a very bad film and a touching one ... about the breaking apart of this reassuring, geometrically perfect, once apparently ageless family of siblings".[[309]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007600-318) Several reviewers stated that some of the performances in the film sounded better than their analogous album tracks.[[310]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007601-319) Describing *Let It Be* as the "only Beatles album to occasion negative, even hostile reviews", Unterberger calls it "on the whole underrated"; he singles out "some good moments of straight hard rock in '[I've Got a Feeling](/wiki/I%27ve_Got_a_Feeling "I've Got a Feeling")' and '[Dig a Pony](/wiki/Dig_a_Pony "Dig a Pony")'" and praises "[Let It Be](/wiki/Let_It_Be_(song) "Let It Be (song)")", "Get Back" and "the folky '[Two of Us](/wiki/Two_of_Us_(Beatles_song) "Two of Us (Beatles song)")', with John and Paul harmonising together".[[311]](#cite_note-320)

McCartney filed suit for the dissolution of the Beatles' contractual partnership on 31 December 1970.[[312]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarry2002139-321) Legal disputes continued long after their break-up and the dissolution was not formalised until 29 December 1974,[[313]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarry2002150-322) when Lennon signed the paperwork terminating the partnership while on vacation with his family at [Walt Disney World](/wiki/Walt_Disney_World "Walt Disney World") Resort in Florida.[[314]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPang2008118-323)

### Post-breakup activities

See also: [Collaborations between ex-Beatles](/wiki/Collaborations_between_ex-Beatles "Collaborations between ex-Beatles")

#### 1970s

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/John_Lennon%2C_1974_%28Restored%29.jpg/120px-John_Lennon%2C_1974_%28Restored%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:John_Lennon,_1974_(Restored).jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/Paul_McCartney_during_a_Wings_concert%2C_1976.jpg/250px-Paul_McCartney_during_a_Wings_concert%2C_1976.jpg)](/wiki/File:Paul_McCartney_during_a_Wings_concert,_1976.jpg)

Lennon in 1974 and McCartney in 1976

Lennon, McCartney, Harrison and Starr all released solo albums in 1970. Their solo records sometimes involved one or more of the other members;[[315]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007601–604-324) Starr's *[Ringo](/wiki/Ringo_(album) "Ringo (album)")* (1973) was the only album to include compositions and performances by all four ex-Beatles, albeit on separate songs. With Starr's participation, Harrison staged [the Concert for Bangladesh](/wiki/The_Concert_for_Bangladesh "The Concert for Bangladesh") in New York City in August 1971.[[316]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007603–604-325) Other than an unreleased jam session in 1974, later [bootlegged](/wiki/Bootleg_recording "Bootleg recording") as *[A Toot and a Snore in '74](/wiki/A_Toot_and_a_Snore_in_%2774 "A Toot and a Snore in '74")*, Lennon and McCartney never recorded together again.[[317]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESandford2006227–229-326)

Two double-LP sets of the Beatles' greatest hits, compiled by Klein, *[1962–1966](/wiki/1962%E2%80%931966 "1962–1966")* and *[1967–1970](/wiki/1967%E2%80%931970 "1967–1970")*, were released in 1973, at first under the Apple Records imprint.[[318]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIngham200669-327) Commonly known as the "Red Album" and "Blue Album", respectively, each has earned a [Multi-Platinum certification](/wiki/Music_recording_certification "Music recording certification") in the US and a Platinum certification in the UK.[[319]](#cite_note-RIAAb-328)[[320]](#cite_note-BPI2009-329) Between 1976 and 1982, EMI/Capitol released a wave of compilation albums without input from the ex-Beatles, starting with the double-disc compilation *[Rock 'n' Roll Music](/wiki/Rock_%27n%27_Roll_Music_(album) "Rock 'n' Roll Music (album)")*.[[321]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESouthallPerry2006109-330) The only one to feature previously unreleased material was *[The Beatles at the Hollywood Bowl](/wiki/The_Beatles_at_the_Hollywood_Bowl "The Beatles at the Hollywood Bowl")* (1977); the first officially issued concert recordings by the group, it contained selections from two shows they played during their 1964 and 1965 US tours.[[322]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIngham200666,_69-331)[[nb 10]](#cite_note-333)

The music and enduring fame of the Beatles were commercially exploited in various other ways, again often outside their creative control. In April 1974, the musical *[John, Paul, George, Ringo ... and Bert](/wiki/John,_Paul,_George,_Ringo_..._and_Bert "John, Paul, George, Ringo ... and Bert")*, written by [Willy Russell](/wiki/Willy_Russell "Willy Russell") and featuring singer [Barbara Dickson](/wiki/Barbara_Dickson "Barbara Dickson"), opened in London. It included, with permission from Northern Songs, eleven Lennon–McCartney compositions and one by Harrison, "[Here Comes the Sun](/wiki/Here_Comes_the_Sun "Here Comes the Sun")". Displeased with the production's use of his song, Harrison withdrew his permission to use it.[[324]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESouthallPerry2006109–110-334) Later that year, the off-Broadway musical *[Sgt. Pepper's Lonely Hearts Club Band on the Road](/wiki/Sgt._Pepper%27s_Lonely_Hearts_Club_Band_on_the_Road "Sgt. Pepper's Lonely Hearts Club Band on the Road")* opened.[[325]](#cite_note-335) *[All This and World War II](/wiki/All_This_and_World_War_II "All This and World War II")* (1976) was an unorthodox nonfiction film that combined newsreel footage with covers of Beatles songs by performers ranging from [Elton John](/wiki/Elton_John "Elton John") and [Keith Moon](/wiki/Keith_Moon "Keith Moon") to the [London Symphony Orchestra](/wiki/London_Symphony_Orchestra "London Symphony Orchestra").[[326]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERodriguez2010306–307-336) The Broadway musical *[Beatlemania](/wiki/Beatlemania_(musical) "Beatlemania (musical)")*, an unauthorised nostalgia revue, opened in early 1977 and proved popular, spinning off five separate touring productions.[[327]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIngham200666–67-337) In 1979, the band sued the producers, settling for several million dollars in damages.[[327]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIngham200666–67-337) *[Sgt. Pepper's Lonely Hearts Club Band](/wiki/Sgt._Pepper%27s_Lonely_Hearts_Club_Band_(film) "Sgt. Pepper's Lonely Hearts Club Band (film)")* (1978), a musical film starring the [Bee Gees](/wiki/Bee_Gees "Bee Gees") and [Peter Frampton](/wiki/Peter_Frampton "Peter Frampton"), was a commercial failure and an "artistic fiasco", according to Ingham.[[328]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIngham200666-338)

Accompanying the wave of Beatles nostalgia and persistent reunion rumours in the US during the 1970s, several entrepreneurs made public offers to the Beatles for a reunion concert.[[329]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchaffner1978169–72-339) Promoter Bill Sargent first offered the Beatles $10 million for a reunion concert in 1974. He raised his offer to $30 million in January 1976 and then to $50 million the following month.[[330]](#cite_note-340)[[331]](#cite_note-341) On 24 April 1976, during a broadcast of *[Saturday Night Live](/wiki/Saturday_Night_Live "Saturday Night Live")*, producer [Lorne Michaels](/wiki/Lorne_Michaels "Lorne Michaels") jokingly offered the Beatles $3,000 to reunite on the show. Lennon and McCartney were watching the live broadcast at Lennon's apartment at [the Dakota](/wiki/The_Dakota "The Dakota") in New York, which was within driving distance of the [NBC studio](/wiki/Studio_8H "Studio 8H") where the show was being broadcast. The former bandmates briefly entertained the idea of going to the studio and surprising Michaels by accepting his offer, but decided not to.[[332]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDoggett2011155-342)

#### 1980s

In December 1980, Lennon was [shot and killed](/wiki/Murder_of_John_Lennon "Murder of John Lennon") outside his New York City apartment by [Mark David Chapman](/wiki/Mark_David_Chapman "Mark David Chapman"), an American Beatles fan. Harrison rewrote the lyrics of his song "[All Those Years Ago](/wiki/All_Those_Years_Ago "All Those Years Ago")" in Lennon's honour. With Starr on drums and McCartney and his wife, [Linda](/wiki/Linda_McCartney "Linda McCartney"), contributing backing vocals, the song was released as a single in May 1981.[[333]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBadman1999284-343) McCartney's own tribute, "[Here Today](/wiki/Here_Today_(Paul_McCartney_song) "Here Today (Paul McCartney song)")", appeared on his *[Tug of War](/wiki/Tug_of_War_(Paul_McCartney_album) "Tug of War (Paul McCartney album)")* album in April 1982.[[334]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarry2002412–413-344) In 1984, Starr co-starred in McCartney's film *[Give My Regards to Broad Street](/wiki/Give_My_Regards_to_Broad_Street_(film) "Give My Regards to Broad Street (film)")*,[[335]](#cite_note-345) and played with McCartney on several of the songs on the [soundtrack](/wiki/Give_My_Regards_to_Broad_Street "Give My Regards to Broad Street").[[336]](#cite_note-346) In 1987, Harrison's *[Cloud Nine](/wiki/Cloud_Nine_(George_Harrison_album) "Cloud Nine (George Harrison album)")* album included "[When We Was Fab](/wiki/When_We_Was_Fab "When We Was Fab")", a song about the Beatlemania era.[[337]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDoggett2009292-347)

When the Beatles' studio albums were released on CD by EMI and Apple Corps in 1987, their catalogue was standardised throughout the world, establishing a canon of the twelve original studio LPs as issued in the UK plus the US LP version of *Magical Mystery Tour*.[[338]](#cite_note-EMI_2009-348) All the remaining material from the singles and EPs that had not appeared on these thirteen studio albums was gathered on the two-volume compilation *[Past Masters](/wiki/Past_Masters "Past Masters")* (1988). Except for the *Red* and *Blue* albums, EMI deleted all its other Beatles compilations – including the *Hollywood Bowl* record – from its catalogue.[[322]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIngham200666,_69-331)

In 1988, the Beatles were inducted into the [Rock and Roll Hall of Fame](/wiki/Rock_and_Roll_Hall_of_Fame "Rock and Roll Hall of Fame"), their first year of eligibility. Harrison and Starr attended the ceremony with Lennon's widow, Yoko Ono, and his two sons, [Julian](/wiki/Julian_Lennon "Julian Lennon") and [Sean](/wiki/Sean_Lennon "Sean Lennon").[[339]](#cite_note-R&R_HoF-349)[[340]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarry2002753-350) McCartney declined to attend, citing unresolved "business differences" that would make him "feel like a complete hypocrite waving and smiling with them at a fake reunion".[[340]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarry2002753-350) The following year, EMI/Capitol settled a decade-long lawsuit filed by the band over royalties, clearing the way to commercially package previously unreleased material.[[341]](#cite_note-351)[[342]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarry2002192-352)

#### 1990s

*[Live at the BBC](/wiki/Live_at_the_BBC_(Beatles_album) "Live at the BBC (Beatles album)")*, the first official release of unissued Beatles performances in 17 years, appeared in 1994.[[343]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarry2000a661–663-353) That same year McCartney, Harrison and Starr collaborated on the *[Anthology](/wiki/The_Beatles_Anthology "The Beatles Anthology")* project. *Anthology* was the culmination of work begun in 1970, when Apple Corps director [Neil Aspinall](/wiki/Neil_Aspinall "Neil Aspinall"), their former road manager and personal assistant, had started to gather material for a documentary with the working title *The Long and Winding Road*.[[344]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarry2000a110–111-354)

During 1995–96, the project yielded a television miniseries, an eight-volume video set and three two-CD/three-LP box sets featuring artwork by [Klaus Voormann](/wiki/Klaus_Voormann "Klaus Voormann"). Documenting their history in the band's own words, the *Anthology* project included the release of several unissued Beatles recordings. Alongside producer [Jeff Lynne](/wiki/Jeff_Lynne "Jeff Lynne"), McCartney, Harrison and Starr also added new instrumental and vocal parts to songs recorded as demos by Lennon in the late 1970s,[[345]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarry2000a111–112,_428,_907–908-355) resulting in the release of two "new" Beatles singles, "[Free as a Bird](/wiki/Free_as_a_Bird "Free as a Bird")" and "[Real Love](/wiki/Real_Love_(Beatles_song) "Real Love (Beatles song)")". A third Lennon demo, "[Now and Then](/wiki/Now_and_Then_(Beatles_song) "Now and Then (Beatles song)")", was also attempted, but abandoned due to the low quality of the recording.[[346]](#cite_note-:0-356) The *Anthology* releases were commercially successful and the television series was viewed by an estimated 400 million people.[[347]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarry2000a111–112-357) A book, *[The Beatles Anthology](/wiki/The_Beatles_Anthology_(book) "The Beatles Anthology (book)")*, followed in October 2000. In 1999, to coincide with the re-release of the 1968 film *Yellow Submarine*, an expanded soundtrack album, *[Yellow Submarine Songtrack](/wiki/Yellow_Submarine_Songtrack "Yellow Submarine Songtrack")*, was issued.[[348]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDoggett2009342-358)

#### 2000s

The Beatles' *[1](/wiki/1_(Beatles_album) "1 (Beatles album)")*, a compilation album of the band's British and American number-one hits, was released on 13 November 2000. It became the fastest-selling album of all time, with 3.6 million sold in its first week[[349]](#cite_note-359) and 13 million within a month.[[350]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould20079-360) It topped albums charts in at least 28 countries.[[351]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESouthallPerry2006204-361) The compilation had sold 31 million copies globally by April 2009.[[352]](#cite_note-362)

Harrison died from [metastatic](/wiki/Metastatic "Metastatic") lung cancer in November 2001.[[353]](#cite_note-363)[[354]](#cite_note-364)[[355]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarry2003119-365) McCartney and Starr were among the musicians who performed at the [Concert for George](/wiki/Concert_for_George "Concert for George"), organised by [Eric Clapton](/wiki/Eric_Clapton "Eric Clapton") and Harrison's widow, [Olivia](/wiki/Olivia_Harrison "Olivia Harrison"). The tribute event took place at the [Royal Albert Hall](/wiki/Royal_Albert_Hall "Royal Albert Hall") on the first anniversary of Harrison's death.[[356]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarry2003138–139-366)

In 2003, *[Let It Be... Naked](/wiki/Let_It_Be..._Naked "Let It Be... Naked")*, a reconceived version of the *Let It Be* album, with McCartney supervising production, was released. One of the main differences from the Spector-produced version was the omission of the original string arrangements.[[357]](#cite_note-367) It was a top-ten hit in both Britain and America. The US album configurations from 1964 to 1965 were released as box sets in 2004 and 2006; *[The Capitol Albums, Volume 1](/wiki/The_Capitol_Albums,_Volume_1 "The Capitol Albums, Volume 1")* and *[Volume 2](/wiki/The_Capitol_Albums,_Volume_2 "The Capitol Albums, Volume 2")* included both stereo and mono versions based on the mixes that were prepared for vinyl at the time of the music's original American release.[[358]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWomack2007100-368)

As a soundtrack for [Cirque du Soleil](/wiki/Cirque_du_Soleil "Cirque du Soleil")'s [Las Vegas](/wiki/Las_Vegas_Strip "Las Vegas Strip") Beatles stage revue, *[Love](/wiki/Love_(Cirque_du_Soleil) "Love (Cirque du Soleil)")*, George Martin and his son [Giles](/wiki/Giles_Martin "Giles Martin") remixed and [blended](/wiki/Mashup_(music) "Mashup (music)") 130 of the band's recordings to create what Martin called "a way of re-living the whole Beatles musical lifespan in a very condensed period".[[359]](#cite_note-369) The show premiered in June 2006 and the *[Love](/wiki/Love_(Beatles_album) "Love (Beatles album)")* album was released that November.[[360]](#cite_note-370) In April 2009, Starr performed three songs with McCartney at a benefit concert held at New York's [Radio City Music Hall](/wiki/Radio_City_Music_Hall "Radio City Music Hall") and organised by McCartney.[[361]](#cite_note-371)

On 9 September 2009, the Beatles' entire back catalogue was reissued following an extensive digital remastering process that lasted four years.[[338]](#cite_note-EMI_2009-348) Stereo editions of all twelve original UK studio albums, along with *Magical Mystery Tour* and the *Past Masters* compilation, were released on compact disc both individually and [as a box set](/wiki/The_Beatles_(The_Original_Studio_Recordings) "The Beatles (The Original Studio Recordings)").[[362]](#cite_note-372) A second collection, *[The Beatles in Mono](/wiki/The_Beatles_in_Mono "The Beatles in Mono")*, included remastered versions of every Beatles album released in true mono along with the original 1965 stereo mixes of *Help!* and *Rubber Soul* (both of which Martin had remixed for the 1987 editions).[[363]](#cite_note-373) *[The Beatles: Rock Band](/wiki/The_Beatles:_Rock_Band "The Beatles: Rock Band")*, a music video game in the *[Rock Band](/wiki/Rock_Band "Rock Band")* series, was issued on the same day.[[364]](#cite_note-CNN_Gross-374) In December 2009, the band's catalogue was officially released in [FLAC](/wiki/FLAC "FLAC") and [MP3](/wiki/MP3 "MP3") format in [a limited edition of 30,000 USB flash drives](/wiki/The_Beatles_(The_Original_Studio_Recordings)#Limited_edition_USB_flash_drive "The Beatles (The Original Studio Recordings)").[[365]](#cite_note-375)

#### 2010s

Owing to a long-running royalty disagreement, the Beatles were among the last major artists to sign deals with online music services.[[366]](#cite_note-376) Residual disagreement emanating from [Apple Corps' dispute with Apple, Inc.](/wiki/Apple_Corps_v_Apple_Computer "Apple Corps v Apple Computer"), [iTunes](/wiki/ITunes "ITunes")' owners, over the use of the name "Apple" was also partly responsible for the delay, although in 2008, McCartney stated that the main obstacle to making the Beatles' catalogue available online was that EMI "want[s] something we're not prepared to give them".[[367]](#cite_note-377) In 2010, the official canon of thirteen Beatles studio albums, *Past Masters* and the "Red" and "Blue" greatest-hits albums were made available on iTunes.[[368]](#cite_note-378)

In 2012, EMI's recorded music operations were sold to [Universal Music Group](/wiki/Universal_Music_Group "Universal Music Group"). In order for Universal Music to acquire EMI, the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union"), for [antitrust](/wiki/Antitrust "Antitrust") reasons, forced EMI to spin off assets including Parlophone. Universal was allowed to keep the Beatles' recorded music catalogue, managed by [Capitol Records](/wiki/Capitol_Records "Capitol Records") under its [Capitol Music Group](/wiki/Capitol_Music_Group "Capitol Music Group") division.[[369]](#cite_note-379) The entire original Beatles album catalogue was also reissued on vinyl in 2012; available either individually or as a box set.[[370]](#cite_note-380)

In 2013, a second volume of BBC recordings, *[On Air – Live at the BBC Volume 2](/wiki/On_Air_%E2%80%93_Live_at_the_BBC_Volume_2 "On Air – Live at the BBC Volume 2")*, was released. That December saw the release of another 59 Beatles recordings on iTunes. The set, titled *[The Beatles Bootleg Recordings 1963](/wiki/The_Beatles_Bootleg_Recordings_1963 "The Beatles Bootleg Recordings 1963")*, had the opportunity to gain a 70-year copyright extension conditional on the songs being published at least once before the end of 2013. Apple Records released the recordings on 17 December to prevent them from going into the public domain and had them taken down from iTunes later that same day. Fan reactions to the release were mixed, with one blogger saying "the hardcore Beatles collectors who are trying to obtain everything will already have these."[[371]](#cite_note-381)[[372]](#cite_note-382)

On 26 January 2014, McCartney and Starr performed together at the [56th Annual Grammy Awards](/wiki/56th_Annual_Grammy_Awards "56th Annual Grammy Awards"), held at the [Staples Center](/wiki/Staples_Center "Staples Center") in Los Angeles.[[373]](#cite_note-383) The following day, *[The Night That Changed America: A Grammy Salute to the Beatles](/wiki/The_Night_That_Changed_America:_A_Grammy_Salute_to_the_Beatles "The Night That Changed America: A Grammy Salute to the Beatles")* television special was taped in the [Los Angeles Convention Center](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Convention_Center "Los Angeles Convention Center")'s West Hall. It aired on 9 February, the exact date of – and at the same time and on the same network as – the original broadcast of the Beatles' first US television appearance on *The Ed Sullivan Show*, 50 years earlier. The special included performances of Beatles songs by current artists as well as by McCartney and Starr, archival footage and interviews with the two surviving ex-Beatles carried out by [David Letterman](/wiki/David_Letterman "David Letterman") at the [Ed Sullivan Theater](/wiki/Ed_Sullivan_Theater "Ed Sullivan Theater").[[374]](#cite_note-384)[[375]](#cite_note-385) In December 2015, the Beatles released their catalogue for streaming on various streaming music services including [Spotify](/wiki/Spotify "Spotify") and [Apple Music](/wiki/Apple_Music "Apple Music").[[376]](#cite_note-386)

In September 2016, the documentary film *[The Beatles: Eight Days a Week](/wiki/The_Beatles:_Eight_Days_a_Week "The Beatles: Eight Days a Week")* was released. Directed by [Ron Howard](/wiki/Ron_Howard "Ron Howard"), it chronicled the Beatles' career during their touring years from 1961 to 1966, from their performances in Liverpool's the Cavern Club in 1961 to their final concert in San Francisco in 1966. The film was released theatrically on 15 September in the UK and the US, and started streaming on [Hulu](/wiki/Hulu "Hulu") on 17 September. It received several awards and nominations, including for Best Documentary at the 70th British Academy Film Awards and the Outstanding Documentary or Nonfiction Special at the 69th Primetime Creative Arts Emmy Awards.[[377]](#cite_note-387) An expanded, remixed and remastered version of *The Beatles at the Hollywood Bowl* was released on 9 September, to coincide with the release of the film.[[378]](#cite_note-388)[[379]](#cite_note-389)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/MaccaO2161218-145_%2846386745121%29.jpg/250px-MaccaO2161218-145_%2846386745121%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:MaccaO2161218-145_(46386745121).jpg)

McCartney and Starr, with [Ronnie Wood](/wiki/Ronnie_Wood "Ronnie Wood") in 2018

On 18 May 2017, [Sirius XM Radio](/wiki/Sirius_XM_Radio "Sirius XM Radio") launched a 24/7 radio channel, The Beatles Channel. A week later, *[Sgt. Pepper's Lonely Hearts Club Band](/wiki/Sgt._Pepper%27s_Lonely_Hearts_Club_Band:_50th_Anniversary_Edition "Sgt. Pepper's Lonely Hearts Club Band: 50th Anniversary Edition")* was reissued with new stereo mixes and unreleased material for the album's 50th anniversary.[[380]](#cite_note-390) Similar box sets were released for *[The Beatles](/wiki/The_Beatles:_50th_Anniversary_Edition "The Beatles: 50th Anniversary Edition")* in November 2018,[[381]](#cite_note-391) and *[Abbey Road](/wiki/Abbey_Road:_50th_Anniversary_Edition "Abbey Road: 50th Anniversary Edition")* in September 2019.[[382]](#cite_note-392) In October 2019, *Abbey Road* returned to number one on the UK Albums Chart. The Beatles broke their own record for the album with the longest gap between topping the charts as *Abbey Road* hit the top spot 50 years after its original release.[[383]](#cite_note-393)

#### 2020s

In November 2021, *[The Beatles: Get Back](/wiki/The_Beatles:_Get_Back "The Beatles: Get Back")*, a documentary directed by [Peter Jackson](/wiki/Peter_Jackson "Peter Jackson") using footage captured for the *Let It Be* film, was released on [Disney+](/wiki/Disney%2B "Disney+") as a three-part [miniseries](/wiki/Miniseries "Miniseries").[[384]](#cite_note-394) A book by the same title was released on 12 October.[[385]](#cite_note-395) A [super deluxe version](/wiki/Let_It_Be:_Special_Edition "Let It Be: Special Edition") of the *[Let It Be](/wiki/Let_It_Be_(album) "Let It Be (album)")* album was released on 15 October.[[386]](#cite_note-396) In January 2022, the album *[Get Back (Rooftop Performance)](/wiki/The_Beatles%27_rooftop_concert#Set_list "The Beatles' rooftop concert")*, consisting of newly mixed audio of [the Beatles' rooftop performance](/wiki/The_Beatles%27_rooftop_concert "The Beatles' rooftop concert"), was released on streaming services.[[387]](#cite_note-397)

In 2022, McCartney and Starr collaborated on [a new recording](/wiki/Let_It_Be_(song)#Dolly_Parton_version "Let It Be (song)") of "[Let It Be](/wiki/Let_It_Be_(song) "Let It Be (song)")" with [Dolly Parton](/wiki/Dolly_Parton "Dolly Parton"), [Peter Frampton](/wiki/Peter_Frampton "Peter Frampton") and [Mick Fleetwood](/wiki/Mick_Fleetwood "Mick Fleetwood"), which was released on Parton's album *[Rockstar](/wiki/Rockstar_(Dolly_Parton_album) "Rockstar (Dolly Parton album)")* in November 2023.[[388]](#cite_note-398)[[389]](#cite_note-:1-399) In October, a [special edition](/wiki/Revolver:_Special_Edition "Revolver: Special Edition") of *Revolver* was released, featuring unreleased demos, studio outtakes, the original mono mix and a new stereo remix using [AI](/wiki/AI "AI") de-mixing technology developed by Peter Jackson's [WingNut Films](/wiki/WingNut_Films "WingNut Films"), which had previously been used to restore audio for the documentary *Get Back*.[[390]](#cite_note-400) New music videos were produced for "[Here, There and Everywhere](/wiki/Here,_There_and_Everywhere "Here, There and Everywhere")" and "[I'm Only Sleeping](/wiki/I%27m_Only_Sleeping "I'm Only Sleeping")", the latter of which won the [Grammy Award for Best Music Video](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Best_Music_Video "Grammy Award for Best Music Video") at the [66th Annual Grammy Awards](/wiki/66th_Annual_Grammy_Awards "66th Annual Grammy Awards").[[391]](#cite_note-401)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e8/McCartneyO2101224_p2_%28167_of_191%29_%2854225060041%29.jpg/330px-McCartneyO2101224_p2_%28167_of_191%29_%2854225060041%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:McCartneyO2101224_p2_(167_of_191)_(54225060041).jpg)

Starr joined McCartney's 19 December 2024 solo show as a guest performer for two Beatles songs.

In June 2023, McCartney announced plans to release "the final Beatles record" later in the year, using Jackson's de-mixing technology to extract Lennon's voice from an old demo of a song that he had written as a solo artist.[[346]](#cite_note-:0-356) The song, "[Now and Then](/wiki/Now_and_Then_(Beatles_song) "Now and Then (Beatles song)")", was released on 2 November 2023.[[392]](#cite_note-NowAndThen2023-402)[[393]](#cite_note-:2-403) Its official music video came out the following day, garnering upwards of 8 million views in its first 12 hours,[[394]](#cite_note-404) as the song arrived on Spotify's rankings as one of the most-streamed current songs.[[393]](#cite_note-:2-403) "Now and Then" debuted simultaneously across music, alternative, news/talk and sports stations. The song's premiere achieved the record for the most radio stations to simulcast a music track.[[395]](#cite_note-405) The song became their first UK number-one single since 1969.[[396]](#cite_note-406) It won the [Grammy Award for Best Rock Performance](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Best_Rock_Performance "Grammy Award for Best Rock Performance") at the [67th Annual Grammy Awards](/wiki/67th_Annual_Grammy_Awards "67th Annual Grammy Awards"), and was also nominated for [Record of the Year](/wiki/Record_of_the_Year "Record of the Year").[[397]](#cite_note-407) The nominations were also historically significant for making "Now and Then" the first [artificial intelligence](/wiki/Artificial_intelligence "Artificial intelligence")-assisted track to be nominated for a Grammy award, and the first to win.[[398]](#cite_note-Digital_Music_News-408)

On 8 May 2024, the 1970 film *[Let It Be](/wiki/Let_It_Be_(1970_film) "Let It Be (1970 film)")* was released on Disney+, following a digital restoration by Jackson's [Park Road Post](/wiki/Park_Road_Post "Park Road Post"); it was the first time it was publicly screened since its original theatrical release.[[399]](#cite_note-409)

## Artistry

See also: [Lennon–McCartney](/wiki/Lennon%E2%80%93McCartney "Lennon–McCartney")

### Development

In *Icons of Rock: An Encyclopedia of the Legends Who Changed Music Forever*, Scott Schinder and Andy Schwartz describe the Beatles' musical evolution:

> In their initial incarnation as cheerful, wisecracking moptops, the Fab Four revolutionised the sound, style, and attitude of popular music and opened rock and roll's doors to a tidal wave of British rock acts. Their initial impact would have been enough to establish the Beatles as one of their era's most influential cultural forces, but they didn't stop there. Although their initial style was a highly original, irresistibly catchy synthesis of early American rock and roll and R&B, the Beatles spent the rest of the 1960s expanding rock's stylistic frontiers, consistently staking out new musical territory on each release. The band's increasingly sophisticated experimentation encompassed a variety of genres, including [folk-rock](/wiki/Folk-rock "Folk-rock"), [country](/wiki/Country_music "Country music"), [psychedelia](/wiki/Psychedelic_music "Psychedelic music"), and [baroque pop](/wiki/Baroque_pop "Baroque pop"), without sacrificing the effortless mass appeal of their early work.[[400]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchinderSchwartz2007160-410)

In *The Beatles as Musicians*, [Walter Everett](/wiki/Walter_Everett_(musicologist) "Walter Everett (musicologist)") describes Lennon and McCartney's contrasting motivations and approaches to composition: "McCartney may be said to have constantly developed – as a means to entertain – a focused musical talent with an ear for counterpoint and other aspects of craft in the demonstration of a universally agreed-upon common language that he did much to enrich. Conversely, Lennon's mature music is best appreciated as the daring product of a largely unconscious, searching but undisciplined artistic sensibility."[[401]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEverett19999-411)

Ian MacDonald describes McCartney as "a natural melodist – a creator of tunes capable of existing apart from their harmony". His melody lines are characterised as primarily "vertical", employing wide, [consonant](/wiki/Consonance_and_dissonance "Consonance and dissonance") intervals which express his "extrovert energy and optimism". Conversely, Lennon's "sedentary, ironic personality" is reflected in a "horizontal" approach featuring minimal, dissonant intervals and repetitive melodies which rely on their harmonic accompaniment for interest: "Basically a realist, he instinctively kept his melodies close to the rhythms and cadences of speech, colouring his lyrics with bluesy tone and harmony rather than creating tunes that made striking shapes of their own."[[402]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacDonald200512-412) MacDonald praises Harrison's lead guitar work for the role his "characterful lines and textural colourings" play in supporting Lennon and McCartney's parts and describes Starr as "the father of modern pop/rock drumming".[[403]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacDonald2005382–383-413)

### Influences

The Beatles' earliest influences include Elvis Presley, [Carl Perkins](/wiki/Carl_Perkins "Carl Perkins"), [Little Richard](/wiki/Little_Richard "Little Richard"), [Chuck Berry](/wiki/Chuck_Berry "Chuck Berry") and [Gene Vincent](/wiki/Gene_Vincent "Gene Vincent").[[404]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarry2000a140,_660,_856–858,_881-414)[[405]](#cite_note-415) During the Beatles' co-residency with Little Richard at the [Star-Club](/wiki/Star-Club "Star-Club") in Hamburg, from April to May 1962, he advised them on the proper technique for performing his songs.[[406]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarry2000a660-416) Of Presley, Lennon said, "Nothing really affected me until I heard Elvis. If there hadn't been Elvis, there would not have been the Beatles."[[407]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarry2000a881-417) Chuck Berry was particularly influential in terms of songwriting and lyrics. Lennon noted, "He was well advanced of his time lyric-wise. We all owe a lot to him."[[408]](#cite_note-418) Other early influences include [Buddy Holly](/wiki/Buddy_Holly "Buddy Holly"), [Eddie Cochran](/wiki/Eddie_Cochran "Eddie Cochran"), [Roy Orbison](/wiki/Roy_Orbison "Roy Orbison"),[[409]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarry2000a289,_526,_830-419) [the Everly Brothers](/wiki/The_Everly_Brothers "The Everly Brothers")[[410]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpitz2005111,_123,_131,_133-420) and [Jerry Lee Lewis](/wiki/Jerry_Lee_Lewis "Jerry Lee Lewis").[[411]](#cite_note-421)

The Beatles continued to absorb influences long after their initial success, often finding new musical and lyrical avenues by listening to their contemporaries, including [Bob Dylan](/wiki/Bob_Dylan "Bob Dylan"), [Smokey Robinson and the Miracles](/wiki/Smokey_Robinson_and_the_Miracles "Smokey Robinson and the Miracles"), [the Who](/wiki/The_Who "The Who"), [Frank Zappa](/wiki/Frank_Zappa "Frank Zappa"), [the Lovin' Spoonful](/wiki/The_Lovin%27_Spoonful "The Lovin' Spoonful"), [the Byrds](/wiki/The_Byrds "The Byrds") and [the Beach Boys](/wiki/The_Beach_Boys "The Beach Boys"), whose 1966 album *[Pet Sounds](/wiki/Pet_Sounds "Pet Sounds")* amazed and inspired McCartney.[[412]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarry2000a99,_217,_357,_1195-422)[[413]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007333–335,_359-423)[[414]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELavezzoli2006147,_150,_162,_169-424)[[415]](#cite_note-425)[[416]](#cite_note-426) Referring to the Beach Boys' creative leader, Martin later stated: "No one made a greater impact on the Beatles than [Brian [Wilson]](/wiki/Brian_Wilson "Brian Wilson")."[[417]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGranata200317-427) Ravi Shankar, with whom Harrison studied for six weeks in India in late 1966, had a significant effect on his musical development during the band's later years.[[418]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELavezzoli2006147,_165,_177-428)

### Genres

Originating as a skiffle group, the Beatles quickly embraced 1950s rock and roll and helped pioneer the Merseybeat genre,[[419]](#cite_note-429) and their repertoire ultimately expanded to include a broad variety of pop music.[[420]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould200730–32,_100–107-430) Reflecting the range of styles they explored, Lennon said of *Beatles for Sale*, "You could call our new one a Beatles country-and-western LP",[[421]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007255-431) while Gould credits *Rubber Soul* as "the instrument by which legions of folk-music enthusiasts were coaxed into the camp of pop".[[422]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007296-432)

[![Two electric guitars, a light brown violin-shaped bass and a darker brown guitar resting against a Vox amplifier](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/Guitarras_de_McCartney_y_Harrison.jpg/250px-Guitarras_de_McCartney_y_Harrison.jpg)](/wiki/File:Guitarras_de_McCartney_y_Harrison.jpg)

A [Höfner "violin" bass guitar](/wiki/H%C3%B6fner_500/1 "Höfner 500/1") and [Gretsch Country Gentleman guitar](/wiki/Gretsch "Gretsch"), models played by McCartney and Harrison, respectively; the [Vox AC30](/wiki/Vox_AC30 "Vox AC30") amplifier behind them is the model the Beatles used during performances in the early 1960s.

Although the 1965 song "Yesterday" was not the first pop record to employ orchestral strings, it marked the group's first recorded use of classical music elements. Gould observes, "The more traditional sound of strings allowed for a fresh appreciation of their talent as composers by listeners who were otherwise allergic to the din of drums and electric guitars."[[423]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007278-433) They continued to experiment with string arrangements to various effect; *Sgt. Pepper*'s "[She's Leaving Home](/wiki/She%27s_Leaving_Home "She's Leaving Home")", for instance, is "cast in the mold of a sentimental Victorian ballad", Gould writes, "its words and music filled with the clichés of musical melodrama".[[424]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007402-434)

The band's stylistic range expanded in another direction with their 1966 B-side "Rain", described by [Martin Strong](/wiki/Martin_Strong "Martin Strong") as "the first overtly psychedelic Beatles record".[[425]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStrong2004108-435) Other psychedelic numbers followed, such as "Tomorrow Never Knows" (recorded before "Rain"), "Strawberry Fields Forever", "[Lucy in the Sky with Diamonds](/wiki/Lucy_in_the_Sky_with_Diamonds "Lucy in the Sky with Diamonds")" and "[I Am the Walrus](/wiki/I_Am_the_Walrus "I Am the Walrus")". The influence of [Indian classical music](/wiki/Indian_classical_music "Indian classical music") was evident in Harrison's "[The Inner Light](/wiki/The_Inner_Light_(song) "The Inner Light (song)")", "[Love You To](/wiki/Love_You_To "Love You To")" and "[Within You Without You](/wiki/Within_You_Without_You "Within You Without You")" – Gould describes the latter two as attempts "to replicate the [raga](/wiki/Raga "Raga") form in miniature".[[426]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007406,_462–463-436)

Innovation was the most striking feature of their creative evolution, according to music historian and pianist Michael Campbell:

> "A Day in the Life" encapsulates the art and achievement of the Beatles as well as any single track can. It highlights key features of their music: the sound imagination, the persistence of tuneful melody and the close coordination between words and music. It represents a new category of song – more sophisticated than pop ... and uniquely innovative. There literally had never before been a song – classical or vernacular – that had blended so many disparate elements so imaginatively.[[427]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECampbell2008196-437)

Philosophy professor Bruce Ellis Benson agrees: "The Beatles ... give us a wonderful example of how such far-ranging influences as Celtic music, rhythm and blues, and country and western could be put together in a new way."[[428]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBenson200343-438)

Author Dominic Pedler describes the way they crossed musical styles:

> Far from moving sequentially from one genre to another (as is sometimes conveniently suggested) the group maintained *in parallel* their mastery of the traditional, catchy chart hit while simultaneously forging rock and dabbling with a wide range of peripheral influences from country to vaudeville. One of these threads was their take on folk music, which would form such essential groundwork for their later collisions with Indian music and philosophy.[[429]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPedler2003256-439)

As the personal relationships between the band members grew increasingly strained, their individual tastes became more apparent. The minimalistic cover artwork for the White Album contrasted with the complexity and diversity of its music, which encompassed Lennon's "[Revolution 9](/wiki/Revolution_9 "Revolution 9")" (whose [musique concrète](/wiki/Musique_concr%C3%A8te "Musique concrète") approach was influenced by Yoko Ono), Starr's [country](/wiki/Country_music "Country music") song "[Don't Pass Me By](/wiki/Don%27t_Pass_Me_By "Don't Pass Me By")", Harrison's [rock ballad](/wiki/Rock_ballad "Rock ballad") "[While My Guitar Gently Weeps](/wiki/While_My_Guitar_Gently_Weeps "While My Guitar Gently Weeps")" and the "[proto-metal](/wiki/Heavy_metal_music#Origins:_late_1960s_and_early_1970s "Heavy metal music") roar" of McCartney's "[Helter Skelter](/wiki/Helter_Skelter_(song) "Helter Skelter (song)")".[[430]](#cite_note-White_Album_AllMusic-440)

### Contribution of George Martin

[![The Beatles with George Martin in the studio in the mid-1960s](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7c/Beatles_and_George_Martin_in_studio_1966.JPG/250px-Beatles_and_George_Martin_in_studio_1966.JPG)](/wiki/File:Beatles_and_George_Martin_in_studio_1966.JPG)

[George Martin](/wiki/George_Martin "George Martin") (second from right) in the studio with the Beatles in the mid-1960s

[George Martin](/wiki/George_Martin "George Martin")'s close involvement in his role as producer made him one of the leading candidates for the informal title of the "[fifth Beatle](/wiki/Fifth_Beatle "Fifth Beatle")".[[431]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarry2000a721-441) He applied his classical musical training in various ways and functioned as "an informal music teacher" to the progressing songwriters, according to Gould.[[432]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007121,_290-442) Martin suggested to a sceptical McCartney that the arrangement of "Yesterday" should feature a string quartet accompaniment, thereby introducing the Beatles to a "hitherto unsuspected world of classical instrumental colour", in MacDonald's description.[[433]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacDonald2005158-443) Their creative development was also facilitated by Martin's willingness to experiment in response to their suggestions, such as adding "something [baroque](/wiki/Baroque_music "Baroque music")" to a particular recording.[[434]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007290-444) In addition to scoring orchestral arrangements for recordings, Martin often performed on them, playing instruments including piano, organ and [brass](/wiki/Brass_instrument "Brass instrument").[[435]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007382,_405,_409,_443,_584-445)

Collaborating with Lennon and McCartney required Martin to adapt to their different approaches to songwriting and recording. MacDonald comments, "while [he] worked more naturally with the conventionally articulate McCartney, the challenge of catering to Lennon's intuitive approach generally spurred him to his more original arrangements".[[436]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacDonald2005238-446) Martin said of the two composers' distinct songwriting styles and his stabilising influence:

> Compared with Paul's songs, all of which seemed to keep in some sort of touch with reality, John's had a psychedelic, almost mystical quality ... John's imagery is one of the best things about his work – 'tangerine trees', 'marmalade skies', 'cellophane flowers' ... I always saw him as an aural [Salvador Dalí](/wiki/Salvador_Dal%C3%AD "Salvador Dalí"), rather than some drug-ridden record artist. On the other hand, I would be stupid to pretend that drugs didn't figure quite heavily in the Beatles' lives at that time ... they knew that I, in my schoolmasterly role, didn't approve ... Not only was I not into it myself, I couldn't see the need for it; and there's no doubt that, if I too had been on dope, *[Pepper](/wiki/Sgt._Pepper%27s_Lonely_Hearts_Club_Band "Sgt. Pepper's Lonely Hearts Club Band")* would never have been the album it was. Perhaps it was the combination of dope and no dope that worked, who knows?[[437]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMartin1979205–206-447)

Harrison echoed Martin's description of his stabilising role:

> I think we just grew through those years together, him as the straight man and us as the loonies; but he was always there for us to interpret our madness – we used to be slightly avant-garde on certain days of the week, and he would be there as the anchor person, to communicate that through the engineers and on to the tape.[[438]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarry2003264-448)

### In the studio

See also: [Recording practices of the Beatles](/wiki/Recording_practices_of_the_Beatles "Recording practices of the Beatles")

Making innovative use of technology while expanding the possibilities of recorded music, the Beatles urged experimentation by Martin and his recording engineers. Seeking ways to put chance occurrences to creative use, accidental guitar feedback, a resonating glass bottle, a tape loaded the wrong way round so that it played backwards – any of these might be incorporated into their music. Their desire to create new sounds on every new recording, combined with Martin's arranging abilities and the studio expertise of EMI staff engineers Norman Smith, [Ken Townsend](/wiki/Ken_Townsend "Ken Townsend") and Geoff Emerick, all contributed significantly to their records from *Rubber Soul* and, especially, *Revolver* onwards.[[439]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHertsgaard1995103-449)

Along with innovative studio techniques such as [sound effects](/wiki/Audio_signal_processing "Audio signal processing"), unconventional microphone placements, tape loops, [double tracking](/wiki/Double_tracking "Double tracking") and [vari-speed](/wiki/Vari-speed "Vari-speed") recording, the Beatles augmented their songs with instruments that were unconventional in rock music at the time. These included string and brass ensembles as well as Indian instruments such as the sitar in "Norwegian Wood" and the [swarmandal](/wiki/Swarmandal "Swarmandal") in "Strawberry Fields Forever".[[440]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacDonald2005212-450) They also used novel electronic instruments such as the Mellotron, with which McCartney supplied the flute voices on the "Strawberry Fields Forever" intro,[[441]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacDonald2005219-451) and the [clavioline](/wiki/Clavioline "Clavioline"), an electronic keyboard that created the unusual oboe-like sound on "[Baby, You're a Rich Man](/wiki/Baby,_You%27re_a_Rich_Man "Baby, You're a Rich Man")".[[442]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacDonald2005259-452)

## Legacy

Main article: [Cultural impact of the Beatles](/wiki/Cultural_impact_of_the_Beatles "Cultural impact of the Beatles")

[![Statue in Liverpool](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/The_Beatles_Statues.jpg/250px-The_Beatles_Statues.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Beatles_Statues.jpg)

The Beatles statue at [Pier Head](/wiki/Pier_Head "Pier Head") in [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool "Liverpool"), their home city

[![Road crossing in London](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1a/Alistar_and_Alfio_from_The_Cairos_cross_Abbey_Road_with_Jesse_Davidson_and_Dj_Sammy_T.jpg/250px-Alistar_and_Alfio_from_The_Cairos_cross_Abbey_Road_with_Jesse_Davidson_and_Dj_Sammy_T.jpg)](/wiki/File:Alistar_and_Alfio_from_The_Cairos_cross_Abbey_Road_with_Jesse_Davidson_and_Dj_Sammy_T.jpg)

[Abbey Road crossing](/wiki/Abbey_Road#Abbey_Road_crossing_and_"Paul_is_dead" "Abbey Road") in London is a popular destination for Beatles fans. In December 2010 it was given [grade II listed](/wiki/Grade_II_listed "Grade II listed") status for its "cultural and historical importance"; the Abbey Road studios themselves had been given similar status earlier in the year.[[443]](#cite_note-453)

Former *[Rolling Stone](/wiki/Rolling_Stone "Rolling Stone")* magazine associate editor [Robert Greenfield](/wiki/Robert_Greenfield "Robert Greenfield") compared the Beatles to [Picasso](/wiki/Picasso "Picasso"), as "artists who broke through the constraints of their time period to come up with something that was unique and original ... [I]n the form of popular music, no one will ever be more revolutionary, more creative and more distinctive ..."[[364]](#cite_note-CNN_Gross-374) The British poet [Philip Larkin](/wiki/Philip_Larkin "Philip Larkin") described their work as "an enchanting and intoxicating hybrid of Negro rock-and-roll with their own adolescent romanticism" and "the first advance in popular music since the War".[[444]](#cite_note-454)

In 1964, the Beatles' arrival in the US is credited with initiating the [album era](/wiki/Album_era "Album era");[[445]](#cite_note-455) the music historian [Joel Whitburn](/wiki/Joel_Whitburn "Joel Whitburn") says that LP sales soon "exploded and eventually outpaced the sales and releases of singles" in the music industry.[[446]](#cite_note-456) They not only sparked the British Invasion of the US,[[447]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEverett1999277-457) they became a globally influential phenomenon as well.[[448]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould20078-458) From the 1920s, the US had dominated popular entertainment culture throughout much of the world, via [Hollywood](/wiki/Cinema_of_the_United_States "Cinema of the United States") films, [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz"), the music of Broadway and [Tin Pan Alley](/wiki/Tin_Pan_Alley "Tin Pan Alley"), and later, the rock and roll that first emerged in [Memphis, Tennessee](/wiki/Memphis,_Tennessee "Memphis, Tennessee").[[350]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould20079-360) The Beatles are regarded as British cultural icons, with young adults from abroad naming the band among a group of people whom they most associated with UK culture.[[449]](#cite_note-459)[[450]](#cite_note-460)

Their musical innovations and commercial success inspired musicians worldwide.[[448]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould20078-458)[[451]](#cite_note-461) Many artists have acknowledged the Beatles' influence and enjoyed chart success with [covers of their songs](/wiki/List_of_cover_versions_of_Beatles_songs "List of cover versions of Beatles songs").[[452]](#cite_note-462) On radio, their arrival marked the beginning of a new era; in 1968 the programme director of New York's [WABC](/wiki/WABC_(AM) "WABC (AM)") radio station forbade his DJs from playing any "pre-Beatles" music, marking the defining line of what would be considered [oldies](/wiki/Oldies "Oldies") on American radio.[[453]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFisher2007198-463) They helped to redefine the album as something more than just a few hits padded out with "[filler](/wiki/Filler_(media) "Filler (media)")",[[454]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEverett199991-464) and they were primary innovators of the modern music video.[[455]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpitz2005609–610-465) The Shea Stadium show with which they opened their [1965 North American tour](/wiki/The_Beatles%27_1965_US_tour "The Beatles' 1965 US tour") attracted an estimated 55,600 people,[[153]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn1992181-159) then the largest audience in concert history; Spitz describes the event as a "major breakthrough ... a giant step toward reshaping the concert business".[[456]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpitz2005576–578-466) Emulation of their clothing and especially their hairstyles, which became a mark of rebellion, had a global impact on fashion.[[111]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007345-114)

According to Gould, the Beatles changed the way people listened to popular music and experienced its role in their lives. From what began as the Beatlemania fad, the group's popularity grew into what was seen as an embodiment of sociocultural movements of the decade. As icons of the [1960s counterculture](/wiki/1960s_counterculture "1960s counterculture"), Gould continues, they became a catalyst for [bohemianism](/wiki/Bohemianism "Bohemianism") and activism in various social and political arenas, fuelling movements such as [women's liberation](/wiki/Feminist_movement "Feminist movement"), [gay liberation](/wiki/Gay_liberation "Gay liberation") and [environmentalism](/wiki/Environmental_movement "Environmental movement").[[457]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould20078–9-467) According to Peter Lavezzoli, after the "more popular than Jesus" controversy in 1966, the Beatles felt considerable pressure to say the right things and "began a concerted effort to spread a message of wisdom and higher consciousness".[[177]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELavezzoli2006176-184)

Other commentators such as Mikal Gilmore and Todd Leopold have traced the inception of their sociocultural impact earlier, interpreting even the Beatlemania period, particularly on their first visit to the US, as a key moment in the development of generational awareness.[[109]](#cite_note-Gimore_(RS_Beatles/Dylan/60s)-112)[[458]](#cite_note-Leopold_(Beatlemania/CNN)-468) Referring to their appearance on *the Ed Sullivan Show* Leopold states: "In many ways, the Sullivan appearance marked the beginning of a cultural revolution ... The Beatles were like aliens dropped into the United States of 1964."[[458]](#cite_note-Leopold_(Beatlemania/CNN)-468) According to Gilmore:

> Elvis Presley had shown us how rebellion could be fashioned into eye-opening style; the Beatles were showing us how style could have the impact of cultural revelation – or at least how a pop vision might be forged into an unimpeachable consensus.[[109]](#cite_note-Gimore_(RS_Beatles/Dylan/60s)-112)

Established in 2009, [Global Beatles Day](/wiki/Global_Beatles_Day "Global Beatles Day") is an annual holiday on 25 June each year that honours and celebrates the ideals of the Beatles.[[459]](#cite_note-469) The date was chosen to commemorate the date the group participated in the [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC") programme *[Our World](/wiki/Our_World_(1967_TV_program) "Our World (1967 TV program)")* in 1967, performing "[All You Need Is Love](/wiki/All_You_Need_Is_Love "All You Need Is Love")" broadcast to an international audience.[[460]](#cite_note-470)

## Awards and achievements

See also: [List of awards and nominations received by the Beatles](/wiki/List_of_awards_and_nominations_received_by_the_Beatles "List of awards and nominations received by the Beatles")
