In 1965, Queen Elizabeth II appointed Lennon, McCartney, Harrison and Starr Members of the [Order of the British Empire](/wiki/Order_of_the_British_Empire "Order of the British Empire") (MBE).[[142]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpitz2005556-148) The Beatles won the 1971 Academy Award for [Best Original Song Score](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Original_Score#1970s "Academy Award for Best Original Score") for the film *[Let It Be](/wiki/Let_It_Be_(1970_film) "Let It Be (1970 film)")* (1970).[[308]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESouthallPerry200696-317) The recipients of seven Grammy Awards[[461]](#cite_note-471) and fifteen [Ivor Novello Awards](/wiki/Ivor_Novello_Awards "Ivor Novello Awards"),[[462]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarry2000a559–560-472) the Beatles have six [Diamond albums](/wiki/Music_recording_certification "Music recording certification"), as well as 20 Multi-Platinum albums, 16 Platinum albums and six Gold albums in the US.[[319]](#cite_note-RIAAb-328) In the UK, the Beatles have four [Multi-Platinum albums](/wiki/Music_recording_certification "Music recording certification"), four [Platinum albums](/wiki/Platinum_albums "Platinum albums"), eight [Gold albums](/wiki/Gold_albums "Gold albums") and one [Silver album](/wiki/Silver_album "Silver album").[[320]](#cite_note-BPI2009-329) They were inducted into the [Rock and Roll Hall of Fame](/wiki/Rock_and_Roll_Hall_of_Fame "Rock and Roll Hall of Fame") in 1988.[[339]](#cite_note-R&R_HoF-349)

The [best-selling music act](/wiki/List_of_best-selling_music_artists "List of best-selling music artists") in history, the Beatles have sold more than 600 million units as of 2012[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=The_Beatles&action=edit).[[463]](#cite_note-bbc41012-473)[[nb 11]](#cite_note-475) From 1991 to 2009 the Beatles sold 57 million albums in United States, according to Nielsen Soundscan.[[465]](#cite_note-476) They have had more [number-one albums on the UK charts](/wiki/List_of_artists_by_number_of_UK_Albums_Chart_number_ones "List of artists by number of UK Albums Chart number ones"), fifteen,[[466]](#cite_note-477) and sold more singles in the UK, 21.9 million, than any other act.[[467]](#cite_note-478) In 2004, *Rolling Stone* ranked the Beatles as the most significant and influential rock music artists of the last 50 years.[[468]](#cite_note-479) They ranked number one on *[Billboard](/wiki/Billboard_(magazine) "Billboard (magazine)")* magazine's list of the all-time most successful Hot 100 artists, released in 2008 to celebrate the US singles chart's 50th anniversary.[[469]](#cite_note-480) As of 2017[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=The_Beatles&action=edit), they hold the record for most number-one hits on the *Billboard* Hot 100, with twenty.[[470]](#cite_note-hot100-481) The [Recording Industry Association of America](/wiki/Recording_Industry_Association_of_America "Recording Industry Association of America") certifies that the Beatles have sold 183 million units in the US, more than any other artist.[[471]](#cite_note-insider-482) They were collectively included in *[Time](/wiki/Time_(magazine) "Time (magazine)")* magazine's compilation of the [20th century's 100 most influential people](/wiki/Time_100:_The_Most_Important_People_of_the_Century "Time 100: The Most Important People of the Century").[[472]](#cite_note-483) In 2014, they received the [Grammy Lifetime Achievement Award](/wiki/Grammy_Lifetime_Achievement_Award "Grammy Lifetime Achievement Award").[[473]](#cite_note-484) In 2004 and 2011, *[Rolling Stone](/wiki/Rolling_Stone "Rolling Stone")* named them the greatest artist of all time.[[474]](#cite_note-485)

In 2007, the Beatles became the first band to feature on a [series of UK postage stamps](/wiki/Great_Britain_commemorative_stamps_2000%E2%80%932009 "Great Britain commemorative stamps 2000–2009") issued by the [Royal Mail](/wiki/Royal_Mail "Royal Mail").[[475]](#cite_note-486) Earlier in 1999, the [United States Postal Service](/wiki/United_States_Postal_Service "United States Postal Service") issued a stamp dedicated to the Beatles and *Yellow Submarine*.[[476]](#cite_note-487)

## Band members

Further information: [List of members of bands featuring members of the Beatles](/wiki/List_of_members_of_bands_featuring_members_of_the_Beatles "List of members of bands featuring members of the Beatles")

**Principal members**

* [John Lennon](/wiki/John_Lennon "John Lennon") – vocals, guitars, keyboards, harmonica, bass (1960–1969; died 1980)
* [Paul McCartney](/wiki/Paul_McCartney "Paul McCartney") – vocals, bass, guitars, keyboards, drums (1960–1970)
* [George Harrison](/wiki/George_Harrison "George Harrison") – guitars, vocals, sitar, keyboards, bass (1960–1970; died 2001)
* [Ringo Starr](/wiki/Ringo_Starr "Ringo Starr") – drums, percussion, vocals (1962–1970)

**Early members**

* [Stuart Sutcliffe](/wiki/Stuart_Sutcliffe "Stuart Sutcliffe") – bass, vocals (1960–1961; died 1962)
* [Pete Best](/wiki/Pete_Best "Pete Best") – drums, vocals (1960–1962)

**Temporary members**

* [Tommy Moore](/wiki/Tommy_Moore_(musician) "Tommy Moore (musician)") (died 1981) and [Norman Chapman](/wiki/Norman_Chapman "Norman Chapman") (died 1995) preceded Best as temporary drummers in the Silver Beetles/Beatles before Best joined in August 1960.[[477]](#cite_note-488)[[478]](#cite_note-489)
* [Chas Newby](/wiki/Chas_Newby "Chas Newby") (died 2023) performed bass with the Beatles for a short span of live shows in December 1960, filling in for Sutcliffe when he remained in Hamburg.[[479]](#cite_note-490)[[480]](#cite_note-491)
* [Jimmie Nicol](/wiki/Jimmie_Nicol "Jimmie Nicol") temporarily replaced Starr during June 1964 for five dates of [the Beatles' 1964 world tour](/wiki/The_Beatles%27_1964_world_tour "The Beatles' 1964 world tour").[[123]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn1992160–161,_163-128)
* [Billy Preston](/wiki/Billy_Preston "Billy Preston") (died 2006) played keyboards with the Beatles during 1969.

## Song catalogue

Until 1969, the Beatles' catalogue was published almost exclusively by [Northern Songs](/wiki/Northern_Songs "Northern Songs"), formed in February 1963 by music publisher [Dick James](/wiki/Dick_James "Dick James") specifically for Lennon and McCartney, though it later acquired songs by other artists. The company was organised with James and his partner, Emmanuel Silver, owning a controlling interest, variously described as 51% or 50% plus one share. McCartney had 20%. Reports again vary concerning Lennon's portion – 19 or 20% – and Brian Epstein's – 9 or 10% – which he received in lieu of a 25% band management fee.[[483]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESouthallPerry200615–17-495)[[484]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENorman1996169–71,_368–369-496)[[485]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrownGaines2002178-497) In 1965, the company went public. 5 million shares were created, of which the original principals retained 3.75 million. James and Silver each received 937,500 shares (18.75% of 5 million); Lennon and McCartney each received 750,000 shares (15%); and Epstein's management company, NEMS Enterprises, received 375,000 shares (7.5%). Of the 1.25 million shares put up for sale, Harrison and Starr each acquired 40,000.[[486]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESouthallPerry200637–38-498) At the time of the stock offering, Lennon and McCartney renewed their three-year publishing contracts, binding them to Northern Songs until 1973.[[487]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESouthallPerry200642-499)

Harrison created [Harrisongs](/wiki/Harrisongs "Harrisongs") to represent his Beatles compositions, but signed a three-year contract with Northern Songs that gave it the copyright to his work until March 1968, which included "[Taxman](/wiki/Taxman "Taxman")" and "[Within You Without You](/wiki/Within_You_Without_You "Within You Without You")".[[488]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESouthallPerry200645-500) The songs on which Starr received co-writing credit before 1968, such as "[What Goes On](/wiki/What_Goes_On_(Beatles_song) "What Goes On (Beatles song)")" and "[Flying](/wiki/Flying_(Beatles_instrumental) "Flying (Beatles instrumental)")", were also Northern Songs copyrights.[[489]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESouthallPerry200646–47-501) Harrison did not renew his contract with Northern Songs when it ended, signing instead with [Apple Publishing](/wiki/Apple_Corps "Apple Corps") while retaining the copyright to his work from that point on. Harrison thus owned the rights to his later Beatles songs such as "[While My Guitar Gently Weeps](/wiki/While_My_Guitar_Gently_Weeps "While My Guitar Gently Weeps")" and "Something". That year, as well, Starr created [Startling Music](/wiki/Startling_Music "Startling Music"), which holds the rights to his Beatles compositions, "Don't Pass Me By" and "[Octopus's Garden](/wiki/Octopus%27s_Garden "Octopus's Garden")".[[490]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESouthallPerry200660–61-502)[[491]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacDonald2005351-503)

In March 1969, James arranged to sell his and his partner's shares of Northern Songs to the British broadcasting company [Associated Television](/wiki/Associated_Television "Associated Television") (ATV), founded by impresario [Lew Grade](/wiki/Lew_Grade "Lew Grade"), without first informing the Beatles. The band then made a bid to gain a controlling interest by attempting to work out a deal with a consortium of London brokerage firms that had accumulated a 14% holding.[[492]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENorman1996369–372-504) The deal collapsed over the objections of Lennon, who declared, "I'm sick of being fucked about by men in suits sitting on their fat arses in [the City](/wiki/City_of_London "City of London")".[[493]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENorman1996372-505) By the end of May, ATV had acquired a majority stake in Northern Songs, controlling nearly the entire Lennon–McCartney catalogue, as well as any future material until 1973.[[494]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiles1998296-506) In frustration, Lennon and McCartney sold their shares to ATV in late October 1969.[[495]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEverett1999236-507)

In 1981, financial losses by ATV's parent company, [Associated Communications Corporation](/wiki/Associated_Communications_Corporation "Associated Communications Corporation") (ACC), led it to attempt to sell its music division. According to authors Brian Southall and Rupert Perry, Grade contacted McCartney, offering ATV Music and Northern Songs for $30 million.[[496]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESouthallPerry2006129-508) According to an account McCartney gave in 1995, he met with Grade and explained he was interested solely in the Northern Songs catalogue if Grade were ever willing to "separate off" that portion of ATV Music. Soon afterwards, Grade offered to sell him Northern Songs for £20 million, giving the ex-Beatle "a week or so" to decide. By McCartney's account, he and Ono countered with a £5 million bid that was rejected.[[497]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESouthallPerry2006130-509) According to reports at the time, Grade refused to separate Northern Songs and turned down an offer of £21–25 million from McCartney and Ono for Northern Songs. In 1982, ACC was acquired in a [takeover](/wiki/Takeover "Takeover") by Australian business magnate [Robert Holmes à Court](/wiki/Robert_Holmes_%C3%A0_Court "Robert Holmes à Court") for £60 million.[[498]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESouthallPerry2006130,_139-510)

In 1985, [Michael Jackson](/wiki/Michael_Jackson "Michael Jackson") purchased ATV for a reported $47.5 million. The acquisition gave him control over the publishing rights to more than 200 Beatles songs, as well as 40,000 other copyrights.[[499]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESouthallPerry2006140,_174,_176-511) In 1995, in a deal that earned him a reported $110 million, Jackson merged his music publishing business with [Sony](/wiki/Sony "Sony"), creating a new company, [Sony/ATV Music Publishing](/wiki/Sony/ATV_Music_Publishing "Sony/ATV Music Publishing"), in which he held a 50% stake. The merger made the new company, then valued at over half a billion dollars, the third-largest music publisher in the world.[[500]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESouthallPerry2006198-512) In 2016, Sony acquired Jackson's share of Sony/ATV from the Jackson estate for $750 million.[[501]](#cite_note-513)

Despite the lack of publishing rights to most of their songs, Lennon's estate and McCartney continue to receive their respective shares of the writers' royalties, which together are 331⁄3% of total commercial proceeds in the US and which vary elsewhere around the world between 50 and 55%.[[502]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESouthallPerry2006195-514) Two of Lennon and McCartney's earliest songs – "Love Me Do" and "P.S. I Love You" – were published by an EMI subsidiary, Ardmore & Beechwood, before they signed with James. McCartney acquired their publishing rights from Ardmore[[503]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESouthallPerry2006192–193-515) in 1978,[[504]](#cite_note-516) and they are the only two Beatles songs owned by McCartney's company MPL Communications.[[505]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarry2002536-517) On 18 January 2017, McCartney filed a suit in the [United States district court](/wiki/United_States_district_court "United States district court") against Sony/ATV Music Publishing seeking to reclaim ownership of his share of the Lennon–McCartney song catalogue beginning in 2018. Under US copyright law, for works published before 1978 the author can reclaim copyrights assigned to a publisher after 56 years.[[506]](#cite_note-518)[[507]](#cite_note-519) McCartney and Sony agreed to a confidential settlement in June 2017.[[508]](#cite_note-520)[[509]](#cite_note-521)

## Notes

1. **[^](#cite_ref-57)** Lennon said of Epstein, "We used to dress how we liked, on and off stage. He'd tell us that jeans were not particularly smart and could we possibly manage to wear proper trousers, but he didn't want us suddenly looking square. He'd let us have our own sense of individuality."[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThe_Beatles200067-44)
2. **[^](#cite_ref-70)** "She Loves You" was surpassed in sales by "[Mull of Kintyre](/wiki/Mull_of_Kintyre_(song) "Mull of Kintyre (song)")", by McCartney's post-Beatles band [Wings](/wiki/Paul_McCartney_and_Wings "Paul McCartney and Wings").[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarry2000a990-69)
3. **[^](#cite_ref-94)** Vee-Jay company president [Ewart Abner](/wiki/Ewart_Abner "Ewart Abner") resigned after it was disclosed he used company funds to cover gambling debts.[[91]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpizer200436-93)
4. **[^](#cite_ref-119)** During the same week in April 1964, a third American Beatles LP joined the two already in circulation; two of the three reached the first spot on the *Billboard* [albums chart](/wiki/Billboard_200 "Billboard 200"), the third peaked at number two.[[115]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn1992351-118)
5. **[^](#cite_ref-126)** Harrison's ringing 12-string inspired [Roger McGuinn](/wiki/Roger_McGuinn "Roger McGuinn"), who obtained his own Rickenbacker and used it to craft the trademark sound of [the Byrds](/wiki/The_Byrds "The Byrds").[[121]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007286–287-125)
6. **[^](#cite_ref-129)** Starr was briefly hospitalised after a [tonsillectomy](/wiki/Tonsillectomy "Tonsillectomy"), and [Jimmy Nicol](/wiki/Jimmie_Nicol "Jimmie Nicol") sat in on drums for the first five dates.[[123]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn1992160–161,_163-128)
7. **[^](#cite_ref-181)** It was not until *[Sgt. Pepper's Lonely Hearts Club Band](/wiki/Sgt._Pepper%27s_Lonely_Hearts_Club_Band "Sgt. Pepper's Lonely Hearts Club Band")* in 1967 that a Beatles album was released with identical track listings in both the UK and the US.[[174]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESouthallPerry200659-180)
8. **[^](#cite_ref-220)** Poirier identified what he termed its "mixed allusiveness": "It's unwise ever to assume that they're doing only one thing or expressing themselves in only one style ... one kind of feeling about a subject isn't enough ... any single induced feeling must often exist within the context of seemingly contradictory alternatives."[[212]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007423–425-219) McCartney said at the time: "We write songs. We know what we mean by them. But in a week someone else says something about it, and you can't deny it. ... You put your own meaning at your own level to our songs."[[212]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould2007423–425-219)
9. **[^](#cite_ref-241)** Epstein had been in a fragile emotional state, stressed by personal troubles. It was speculated that he was concerned that the band might not renew his management contract, due to expire in October, over discontent with his supervision of business matters, particularly regarding [Seltaeb](/wiki/Seltaeb "Seltaeb"), the company that handled their US merchandising rights.[[232]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrownGaines2002227–228-240)
10. **[^](#cite_ref-333)** The band unsuccessfully attempted to block the 1977 release of *[Live! at the Star-Club in Hamburg, Germany; 1962](/wiki/Live!_at_the_Star-Club_in_Hamburg,_Germany;_1962 "Live! at the Star-Club in Hamburg, Germany; 1962")*. The independently issued album compiled recordings made during the group's [Hamburg residency](/wiki/The_Beatles_in_Hamburg "The Beatles in Hamburg"), taped on a basic recording machine using only one microphone.[[323]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarry2000a124–126-332)
11. **[^](#cite_ref-475)** Another estimate gives total international sales of over 1 billion units,[[350]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould20079-360) a figure based on EMI's statement and recognised by *[Guinness World Records](/wiki/Guinness_World_Records "Guinness World Records")*.[[464]](#cite_note-474)
12. **[^](#cite_ref-494)** The *Past Masters* compilation of singles and EP tracks was originally released as two separate albums, Volumes One and Two in 1988. However, they were later merged into one compilation.[[482]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewisohn1988201-493)
